text:
# Extending Unsupervised Anomaly Detection to Europa Surface Imagery: A Proposal to Deep Science Ventures

**PI: [Name] | Affiliation: [Institution] | Requested: £185,000 over 18 months**

---

## The Problem I Actually Want to Solve

I've spent the last fourteen months building an unsupervised anomaly detection pipeline for orbital imagery, trained initially on Lunar Reconnaissance Orbiter NAC data. The system works. It flags geologically interesting surface features — fresh impacts, pyroclastic vents, anomalous albedo regions — without labeled training data, using a variational autoencoder architecture with a modified reconstruction-loss scoring mechanism. On a held-out set of 14,200 LRO tiles, it achieves a 91.3% agreement rate with expert-flagged anomalies at a false positive rate of 4.7%. I'm genuinely proud of that false positive number. It took me five months of dead ends with standard β-VAE tuning before I switched to a dynamic perceptual loss weighting scheme that actually respected the spatial frequency structure of regolith textures.

Now I want to point this system at Europa.

## Why Europa, Why Now

The Europa Clipper mission will return an estimated 4.8 terabits of surface imagery starting in 2031. The Europa Imaging System (EIS) will achieve resolutions down to 0.5 m/pixel during close flybys. No human team will manually review that volume. More importantly, we don't fully know what we're looking for — the chaos terrain morphologies, potential cryovolcanic features, and surface-subsurface exchange signatures on Europa have no comprehensive taxonomy. This is exactly the regime where unsupervised methods should lead, not follow, the science.

But you can't just retrain on Europa data that doesn't exist yet. This is the core technical challenge.

## What I'm Proposing

I want to build a domain-adapted version of my pipeline that bridges the lunar-to-Europa gap using three strategies:

**1. Synthetic Europa terrain generation.** I'll use existing Galileo SSI mosaics (limited, noisy, ~200 m/pixel) combined with physics-informed surface modeling — ice rheology constraints, tidal fracture mechanics — to generate plausible high-resolution Europa surface tiles. I've already run preliminary tests: my VAE latent space transfers surprisingly well to synthetic icy terrains when I freeze the encoder's first four convolutional blocks and retrain the decoder. Reconstruction loss drops from 0.34 to 0.11 within 80 epochs.

**2. Spectral domain adaptation.** Europa's surface reflectance is nothing like the Moon's. I'm implementing a channel-wise normalization layer conditioned on surface composition priors (water ice vs. irradiated salts vs. silicate contaminants) so the anomaly scoring isn't confounded by expected spectral variation.

**3. Hierarchical anomaly taxonomy.** My lunar system outputs a single anomaly score. For Europa, I want to decompose this into morphological, textural, and contextual anomaly subscores. A chaos terrain margin that looks texturally normal but sits in a geometrically impossible orientation should score differently than a region with anomalous surface roughness. This matters for prioritizing follow-up observations during the mission's limited flyby windows.

## What I'm Not Proposing

I'm not building a classifier. I'm not claiming I'll detect biosignatures. I'm building infrastructure — a system that says *this patch of Europa is statistically weird, and here's specifically how it's weird* — so that when Clipper data arrives, the planetary science community isn't drinking from a firehose.

## Budget Justification (Brief)

£185,000 covers one postdoc (£120k with overhead), GPU compute on JASMIN (£35k), and two workshops bringing in Europa geologists to stress-test the anomaly outputs against their intuitions (£30k). The geology workshops aren't optional — my lunar system improved by six points on agreement rate after I incorporated feedback from impact specialists who told me my texture features were ignoring ejecta ray asymmetries. Domain experts make the unsupervised system better, paradoxically.

I've built the thing that works on the Moon. Let me make it ready for the ocean world.
corpus_texts:
# The Tiling Artifact Problem That Ate Three Weeks of My Life

So here's something nobody warns you about when you're building anomaly detection for orbital imagery: your model will absolutely learn to detect your preprocessing instead of actual surface anomalies.

I discovered this the hard way in March when I noticed our unsupervised pipeline was flagging a suspicious grid pattern across Jezero Crater mosaics. Perfect rectangular clusters of "anomalies," spaced at exactly 256×256 pixel intervals. My heart sank. The model had learned to detect tile boundary artifacts from our image stitching step.

The core issue was subtle. When we slice large orbital scenes into tiles for the autoencoder, the edge pixels get slightly different normalization statistics than center pixels. We're talking tiny — mean intensity differences of like 0.003 in reflectance units. But our variational autoencoder was sensitive enough to pick that up as reconstruction error, and the anomaly scores along tile boundaries were consistently elevated. Our mean boundary reconstruction error was 0.0147 versus 0.0089 for tile interiors. That gap was enough to push roughly 12% of boundary pixels above our detection threshold.

I tried three things that didn't work: overlapping tiles with averaging (introduced blurring that killed legitimate detections), post-hoc masking of boundary regions (lost real anomalies near edges — including a genuinely interesting albedo feature in Syrtis Major that I'm still mad about), and per-tile normalization (created its own new artifacts, obviously in retrospect).

What actually worked was embarrassingly simple. I added a learned positional bias term to the decoder that explicitly models the tile position of each pixel. Basically I gave the autoencoder permission to know where it is within a tile, so it could account for the boundary effect itself rather than flagging it. Reconstruction error at boundaries dropped to 0.0098 — nearly matching interior pixels — and our false positive rate fell from 23% to about 7% overnight.

The lesson I keep relearning: in unsupervised anomaly detection, your model is only as good as your ability to make the "normal" background truly boring. Every preprocessing decision is a potential false signal. I now audit every upstream change by computing anomaly score distributions stratified by processing metadata before I even look at geological results.

The tile bias trick isn't novel. But losing three weeks to it definitely was.

# The Farside Thermal Anomaly That Almost Got Filtered Out

Last March I nearly deleted what turned out to be the most interesting result of my entire project.

I've been running my unsupervised anomaly detection pipeline on Lunar Reconnaissance Orbiter Diviner radiometer data — about 14 months now of tuning, breaking things, retuning. The system flags thermal inertia outliers on the lunar surface using a variational autoencoder with a reconstruction error threshold I'd calibrated at 3.2σ after way too many iterations. Anything above that gets tagged for review.

The problem is that my initial spatial filtering was too aggressive. I was throwing out anything smaller than 4×4 pixel clusters because single-pixel anomalies are almost always instrument artifacts or cosmic ray hits. Reasonable, right? Except in late March I was debugging a memory leak and happened to visualize the pre-filtered detections for a Diviner swath over the farside highlands, around 167°E, 38°S. There was a constellation of 2×2 and 3×3 pixel clusters — seventeen of them — scattered across a ~40 km region that individually fell below my size cutoff but collectively formed a pattern I'd never seen before.

These spots showed nightside brightness temperatures running 8-11 K above the surrounding regolith at local times around 2:00 AM. Their thermal decay curves were wrong. The surrounding terrain followed the standard t^(-1/4) cooling you'd expect for fine particulate regolith, but these anomalies decayed closer to t^(-1/6), suggesting effective thermal inertia values around 390 J·m⁻²·K⁻¹·s⁻¹/² — roughly 5× the highland average of ~80.

That's rock. Exposed bedrock or very blocky deposits. But here's what surprised me: this region has no obvious association with any mapped crater younger than ~2 Ga. There's no fresh impact that should have excavated coherent rock to the surface. The spatial distribution doesn't match typical ejecta patterns either — it's too regular, almost grid-like, which frankly made me paranoid about systematic instrument error for weeks.

I've since confirmed the signal persists across six independent orbital passes spanning two years. It's real.

I don't have an explanation yet. My best guess involves subsurface fracture networks from an ancient basin-forming event channeling heat transport, but I'm honestly not confident. What I *am* confident about: my original 4×4 minimum cluster size was hiding things from me. I've since dropped it to 2×2 with an added spatial correlation check. My false positive rate went from 0.3% to 1.7%, which is manageable.

Sometimes the interesting stuff lives right below your filtering threshold.

text:
# Cross-Sensor Calibration of OASIS: Quantifying Anomaly Detection Stability Across Orbital Imaging Platforms

**PI: [Name] | Requested: £87,500 | Duration: 14 months**

---

## The Problem I Keep Running Into

Over the past year I've built OASIS (Orbital Anomaly Statistical Identification System), an unsupervised pipeline that flags geomorphological anomalies in orbital imagery without requiring labeled training sets. On HiRISE data, it works well — I'm seeing a precision-at-50 of 0.81 for novel surface features and a false discovery rate under 12% when validated against manually catalogued RSL and fresh impact sites. I'm genuinely proud of those numbers.

But here's what keeps me up at night: I have no idea how badly those numbers degrade when I switch cameras.

Every time I've tried to naively pass CaSSIS or CTX imagery through the same pipeline, the anomaly rankings shift in ways I can't fully explain. Some of it is obvious — CaSSIS gives me 4.6 m/px versus HiRISE's 0.25 m/px, so the texture features my autoencoder learned are encoding fundamentally different spatial frequencies. Some of it is less obvious. I ran a quick comparison last spring on Jezero crater coverage from all three instruments and found that my reconstruction residual distributions didn't just shift — their kurtosis changed sign. The pipeline wasn't just less accurate on CaSSIS; it was failing in a structurally different way. That scared me enough to write this proposal.

## What I Want To Do

I'm requesting funding for a systematic calibration study comparing OASIS performance across HiRISE, CTX, and CaSSIS for three target classes: fresh impacts, seasonal albedo transients, and slope-associated flow features. Specifically:

**Phase 1 (months 1–5):** Build co-registered evaluation mosaics for 40 sites where all three cameras have overlapping coverage at comparable solar incidence angles. This is the tedious part and the part nobody wants to fund, which is exactly why I'm asking. I'll use my existing SPICE-kernel alignment workflow but will need a dedicated research assistant for manual QA — automated co-registration fails silently on roughly 15% of CaSSIS stamps due to jitter artifacts.

**Phase 2 (months 6–10):** Run OASIS on each sensor's data independently, then characterize how five key metrics — reconstruction MSE, anomaly rank correlation, precision-at-k, spatial agreement (IoU of flagged regions), and residual kurtosis — vary as a function of sensor, resolution, and surface type. I want a lookup table of expected degradation, not just a vague sense that "it works less well."

**Phase 3 (months 11–14):** Test two mitigation strategies I've been prototyping: resolution-adaptive latent space rescaling (promising — improved rank correlation from 0.43 to 0.67 in a pilot on 6 CTX scenes) and a sensor-conditional prior that modulates the anomaly threshold based on instrument noise characteristics. I'll publish whichever works. I'll also publish if neither works, because the community needs that information.

## Why This Matters Beyond My Pipeline

Unsupervised anomaly detection is becoming a real tool for planetary exploration, not just a conference demo. ESA's upcoming EnVision mission and the push toward autonomous onboard science prioritization both assume that these methods generalize. Right now, nobody has published rigorous cross-sensor transfer numbers for any unsupervised planetary detection system. We're all just hoping it works. I'd like to replace hope with a table of coefficients.

## Budget Summary

- Research assistant (12 months, 0.8 FTE): £42,000
- Cloud compute (GPU instances for retraining + inference across ~11TB of imagery): £18,500
- PI summer salary buyout (2 months): £22,000
- Travel for presenting results at LPSC and EPSC: £5,000

I know this isn't a flashy proposal. It's a calibration study. But calibration is what separates a prototype from a tool, and I think OASIS is ready to become a tool — I just need to prove it.

text:
# Grant Application: OrbitalAnomaly — An Open-Source Toolkit for Unsupervised Anomaly Detection in Planetary Imagery

**PI: [Name] | Submitted to Deep Science Ventures**

---

## The Problem I Actually Solved (and Why Nobody Can Use It)

Over the past fourteen months, I built an unsupervised anomaly detection pipeline — what I internally call SIGIL — that flags morphological anomalies in orbital imagery without labeled training data. It works. On our HiRISE validation set, it achieves a mean anomaly capture rate of 87.3% at a false positive density of 0.41 per 10⁴ km², which outperforms the best supervised baseline we benchmarked against (JPL's 2021 classifier, 79.1% capture rate) despite never seeing a single labeled example.

The problem is that SIGIL lives as 23,000 lines of research code tangled around my specific data pipeline, my specific GPU cluster, and my specific bad decisions from month three that I never refactored. No other lab can use it. I've had requests from four groups — two working on Venusian radar mosaics, one on Europa Clipper prep, one on lunar volatile mapping — and every time I have to say "I'll send you the paper" instead of "here's the tool."

That's the gap this proposal fills.

## What I Want to Build

**OrbitalAnomaly**: a modular, open-source Python toolkit that extracts the methodological core of SIGIL into something a planetary science lab can install, configure, and run on their own orbital datasets in under a day.

The three architectural components:

1. **Tiling & spectral normalization engine.** This handles the unglamorous but critical preprocessing — adaptive tiling that respects instrument-specific point spread functions, cross-strip radiometric normalization, and the selective Laplacian decomposition step that turned out to be the single biggest contributor to our performance (we gained 11 percentage points in capture rate when we added it, which frankly surprised me).

2. **Multi-scale variational autoencoder with attention-gated skip connections.** This is the anomaly detection core. The key architectural choice — and the one I'd fight someone over — is that we do NOT use reconstruction error alone as the anomaly score. We combine it with a learned prior divergence metric weighted by local texture entropy. Reconstruction-error-only approaches plateau around 72% capture in our ablations. The combined score is what gets us to 87%.

3. **Calibration and export layer.** Anomaly scores are meaningless without instrument-aware statistical calibration. This module fits a generalized Pareto distribution to the tail of the score distribution and outputs georeferenced anomaly maps with per-pixel confidence intervals. It also exports directly to GIS formats, because I've learned that if a tool doesn't meet scientists where they already work, it dies.

## Budget Justification (£85,000 over 12 months)

- **Software engineer (0.7 FTE): £52,000.** I am not the right person to refactor my own code. I tried. The result was worse.
- **Documentation, testing, CI/CD: £13,000.** This includes compute costs for automated regression testing across three reference datasets.
- **Community workshops (2x virtual, 1x in-person at LPSC): £10,000.**
- **PI time (0.15 FTE summer buyout): £10,000.**

## Why This Matters Beyond My Lab

Planetary science is entering a data deluge — JUICE, Europa Clipper, Dragonfly — and our community's anomaly detection methodology is still largely "a graduate student looks at images." Unsupervised methods should be the first pass on every new dataset. But right now the barrier isn't algorithmic; it's engineering. OrbitalAnomaly eliminates that barrier.

Everything will be released under Apache 2.0. Every architectural decision will be documented with the ablation data that justified it, including the dead ends. Science reproduces better when we share our wrong turns too.

text:
# Subsurface Ice Detection on Mars via Unsupervised Anomaly Detection Applied to HiRISE Imagery

**PI: [Name] | Requested: £185,000 over 18 months**

---

## The Problem I Actually Want to Solve

We have roughly 700,000 HiRISE images and counting. Each one is massive — up to 20,000 × 120,000 pixels at 25–50 cm/pixel. The community has done extraordinary targeted work identifying exposed ice in fresh impact craters, expanded scalloped terrain, and sublimation features. But the search paradigm is still fundamentally supervised: we know what we're looking for, we train on known examples, and we find more of the same. That's useful. It's also how you miss things.

I've spent the last fourteen months building an unsupervised anomaly detection pipeline — originally for Lunar Reconnaissance Orbiter NAC data — that inverts this logic. Instead of asking "does this look like ice?" it asks "does this look like anything I've seen before?" Features that score high on our anomaly metric (we use a modified Mahalanobis distance in a learned latent space, which outperformed isolation forests and variational autoencoder reconstruction error by 12–18% in our receiver-blind benchmarks on LRO data) get flagged for inspection. On the Moon, this system independently recovered known pyroclastic vents, fresh impacts, and — this surprised us — a class of subtle albedo boundaries in mare terrain that turned out to correspond to buried lava tube margins confirmed by GRAIL gravity data. That last category wasn't in any training set because we didn't have a training set.

## What I Want to Do with HiRISE

I want to run this system across the mid-latitude HiRISE archive (roughly 40°–60° N/S, where theoretical models and radar data suggest widespread subsurface ice within meters of the surface) and look for spatial anomalies that may indicate ice-related geomorphology we haven't catalogued. Specific signatures I expect to recover include polygonal terrain at scales below current survey completeness, lobate flow features, and fresh exposures in new impacts. But the point is explicitly to also find things I can't list here.

The technical adaptation is nontrivial but scoped. HiRISE has different noise characteristics than NAC — the TDI architecture creates along-track correlated noise that our current preprocessing doesn't handle. We burned about three months learning this lesson on LRO and I won't repeat it. I'm budgeting a dedicated postdoc for the first four months solely on radiometric adaptation and validation against the well-characterized HiRISE test patches in Jezero and Gale.

## Why This Matters Beyond Mars Science

The subsurface ice distribution on Mars is a first-order input to both astrobiology site selection and ISRU planning for human exploration. Current maps (Piqueux et al. 2019, Morgan et al. 2021) rely heavily on thermal inertia, SHARAD radar, and neutron spectroscopy — all at resolutions orders of magnitude coarser than HiRISE. If we can flag morphological ice indicators at 50 cm/pixel and cross-reference them with these coarser datasets, we produce something genuinely new: a multi-resolution ice confidence map with spatial granularity relevant to landing site selection.

## What I'm Not Proposing

I'm not proposing a confirmatory survey. I'm not building a classifier. I'm deliberately avoiding the temptation to hand-label 5,000 ice examples and train a detector, because that approach has been done competently by several groups and it finds what you already know. The tradeoff is interpretability — anomaly detection surfaces weird things, and some of them will be detector artifacts or geological features irrelevant to ice. I'm building in a rigorous triage protocol (postdoc + two graduate students, structured blind review) to handle this.

**Deliverables:** Open-source pipeline, anomaly catalog with confidence scores, and a mid-latitude ice indicator map registered to MOLA topography. All public within six months of project end.

I think this is how you find what you weren't looking for.

# Grant Application: Deep Science Ventures — Collaborative Research Fund

**PI:** Dr. Kira Vasquez-Okonkwo, Planetary Surface Dynamics Group
**Co-I:** Dr. Lena Marchetti, Mineral Spectroscopy Laboratory, ETH Zürich
**Requested Amount:** £187,000 over 18 months
**Title:** Coupling Unsupervised Orbital Anomaly Detection with Ground-Truth Spectroscopic Validation for Compositional Discovery on Rocky Bodies

---

## The Problem We Actually Have

Over the past fourteen months I built CARDINAL — a variational autoencoder pipeline that flags morphological and albedo anomalies in orbital imagery without requiring labeled training sets. It works. On a holdout corpus of 1.2 million HiRISE tiles, CARDINAL achieves a reconstruction-residual AUC of 0.943 and, more importantly, surfaces genuinely interesting things: it independently re-detected recurring slope lineae in Garni crater, flagged three previously uncatalogued albedo anomalies in Syrtis Major, and found a class of small-scale polygonal terrain in Acidalia that I still can't fully explain.

But here's what CARDINAL cannot tell me: *what any of these anomalies are made of*. A bright patch is a bright patch. I have pixel-level anomaly scores and no compositional attribution. I spent two months trying to bolt CRISM hyperspectral overlays onto the pipeline as an additional input channel, and the results were, frankly, garbage — the spatial resolution mismatch (6 m/px vs. 18 m/px) introduced artifacts that the decoder learned to treat as features rather than noise. My false-positive rate tripled. Dead end.

What I need is not another clever resampling trick. I need a spectroscopy collaborator who can work backward from my detections to mineral-phase identities, and that is Dr. Marchetti's lab.

## What We Propose

Phase 1 (months 1–6): Marchetti's group will perform targeted VNIR/SWIR reflectance analysis on the 312 highest-confidence CARDINAL detections, using CRISM along-track oversampled (ATO) data where available and OMEGA coverage elsewhere. Her lab has a proven deconvolution pipeline (Marchetti et al., 2022) that resolves olivine–pyroxene mixtures to ±4 wt% in laboratory analogs. We will build a shared detection–composition catalog with standardized metadata.

Phase 2 (months 4–14, overlapping): Using that catalog, I will train a lightweight supervised branch that sits downstream of CARDINAL's anomaly scorer — essentially a compositional hypothesis module. The key insight is that we are *not* asking spectroscopy to fix the detector. We are asking it to *interpret* the detector's outputs. This respects the spatial resolution each instrument was designed for and avoids the fusion artifacts that killed my earlier attempt.

Phase 3 (months 12–18): Validation against three independent benchmarks — the MICA mineral map library, PDS Geoscience Node spot spectra, and Marchetti's own Oman ophiolite field analogs. We aim for top-3 compositional class accuracy ≥0.78 on out-of-distribution tiles, which would be a first for any pipeline operating at HiRISE spatial scale.

## Why This Matters Beyond Mars

The architecture is sensor-agnostic. CARDINAL already runs on LRO NAC lunar data with minor retuning (AUC 0.921). If the compositional branch generalizes, we have a discovery engine that can be pointed at any rocky body with overlapping imaging and spectral coverage — asteroids from OSIRIS-APEX, Venus from EnVision, the Galilean moons from JUICE. The anomaly detector says *"look here."* The spectroscopy module says *"here's what you might be seeing."* Together they close the loop that neither instrument team closes alone.

## Budget Justification (Summary)

- Postdoc (shared, 18 months): £109,000
- Marchetti lab instrument time & ATO data processing: £41,000
- Compute (GPU cluster, cloud burst for retraining): £24,000
- Travel for two joint analysis workshops: £8,000
- Open-access publication fees

# The Tiling Artifact Problem That Ate Three Weeks of My Life

So here's something nobody warns you about when you're building anomaly detection for orbital imagery: your model will absolutely learn to detect your preprocessing instead of actual surface anomalies.

I discovered this the hard way in March when I noticed our unsupervised pipeline was flagging a suspicious grid pattern across Jezero Crater mosaics. Perfect rectangular clusters of "anomalies," spaced at exactly 256×256 pixel intervals. My heart sank. The model had learned to detect tile boundary artifacts from our image stitching step.

The core issue was subtle. When we slice large orbital scenes into tiles for the autoencoder, the edge pixels get slightly different normalization statistics than center pixels. We're talking tiny — mean intensity differences of like 0.003 in reflectance units. But our variational autoencoder was sensitive enough to pick that up as reconstruction error, and the anomaly scores along tile boundaries were consistently elevated. Our mean boundary reconstruction error was 0.0147 versus 0.0089 for tile interiors. That gap was enough to push roughly 12% of boundary pixels above our detection threshold.

I tried three things that didn't work: overlapping tiles with averaging (introduced blurring that killed legitimate detections), post-hoc masking of boundary regions (lost real anomalies near edges — including a genuinely interesting albedo feature in Syrtis Major that I'm still mad about), and per-tile normalization (created its own new artifacts, obviously in retrospect).

What actually worked was embarrassingly simple. I added a learned positional bias term to the decoder that explicitly models the tile position of each pixel. Basically I gave the autoencoder permission to know where it is within a tile, so it could account for the boundary effect itself rather than flagging it. Reconstruction error at boundaries dropped to 0.0098 — nearly matching interior pixels — and our false positive rate fell from 23% to about 7% overnight.

The lesson I keep relearning: in unsupervised anomaly detection, your model is only as good as your ability to make the "normal" background truly boring. Every preprocessing decision is a potential false signal. I now audit every upstream change by computing anomaly score distributions stratified by processing metadata before I even look at geological results.

The tile bias trick isn't novel. But losing three weeks to it definitely was.

# The Farside Thermal Anomaly That Almost Got Filtered Out

Last March I nearly deleted what turned out to be the most interesting result of my entire project.

I've been running my unsupervised anomaly detection pipeline on Lunar Reconnaissance Orbiter Diviner radiometer data — about 14 months now of tuning, breaking things, retuning. The system flags thermal inertia outliers on the lunar surface using a variational autoencoder with a reconstruction error threshold I'd calibrated at 3.2σ after way too many iterations. Anything above that gets tagged for review.

The problem is that my initial spatial filtering was too aggressive. I was throwing out anything smaller than 4×4 pixel clusters because single-pixel anomalies are almost always instrument artifacts or cosmic ray hits. Reasonable, right? Except in late March I was debugging a memory leak and happened to visualize the pre-filtered detections for a Diviner swath over the farside highlands, around 167°E, 38°S. There was a constellation of 2×2 and 3×3 pixel clusters — seventeen of them — scattered across a ~40 km region that individually fell below my size cutoff but collectively formed a pattern I'd never seen before.

These spots showed nightside brightness temperatures running 8-11 K above the surrounding regolith at local times around 2:00 AM. Their thermal decay curves were wrong. The surrounding terrain followed the standard t^(-1/4) cooling you'd expect for fine particulate regolith, but these anomalies decayed closer to t^(-1/6), suggesting effective thermal inertia values around 390 J·m⁻²·K⁻¹·s⁻¹/² — roughly 5× the highland average of ~80.

That's rock. Exposed bedrock or very blocky deposits. But here's what surprised me: this region has no obvious association with any mapped crater younger than ~2 Ga. There's no fresh impact that should have excavated coherent rock to the surface. The spatial distribution doesn't match typical ejecta patterns either — it's too regular, almost grid-like, which frankly made me paranoid about systematic instrument error for weeks.

I've since confirmed the signal persists across six independent orbital passes spanning two years. It's real.

I don't have an explanation yet. My best guess involves subsurface fracture networks from an ancient basin-forming event channeling heat transport, but I'm honestly not confident. What I *am* confident about: my original 4×4 minimum cluster size was hiding things from me. I've since dropped it to 2×2 with an added spatial correlation check. My false positive rate went from 0.3% to 1.7%, which is manageable.

Sometimes the interesting stuff lives right below your filtering threshold.

# Grant Application: Urban Air Quality Sensor Network Expansion

**Submitted to:** Green Horizons Climate Fund
**Project Title:** Scaling Community-Based Air Quality Monitoring Across Three Cities
**Requested Amount:** $187,500
**Project Duration:** 18 months
**Applicant:** [Organization Name]

---

## Project Summary

We're requesting $187,500 to expand our existing 50-node urban air quality sensor network to 200 nodes across Detroit, MI; Richmond, VA; and Fresno, CA. Our current deployment — running since 2022 — has already identified six previously undocumented pollution hotspots near schools and public housing. This expansion puts hyperlocal PM2.5 and PM10 data directly into the hands of communities that EPA's sparse regulatory monitoring network consistently misses.

## The Problem

EPA operates roughly 4,000 monitoring stations nationwide. That's one station per 80,000 people on average — and those stations rarely sit in environmental justice communities. In our three target cities, entire zip codes have zero continuous particulate monitoring. Residents know the air is bad. They need the data to prove it.

## What We've Built So Far

Our 50 existing nodes use Plantower PMS5003 sensors paired with BME280 temperature/humidity modules on custom ESP32 boards. Each node costs approximately $85 in materials, reports data every 90 seconds via WiFi or LoRaWAN, and pushes to a public dashboard. We co-locate 10% of our sensors within 100 meters of Federal Reference Method (FRM) monitors for ongoing calibration validation. After applying EPA's correction factors and our own humidity-adjusted regression models, our PM2.5 readings hold an R² of 0.91 against FRM data across seasonal conditions.

We didn't get here overnight. Early deployments taught us hard lessons about sensor drift — PMS5003 units lose accuracy after roughly 8,000 hours in high-humidity environments. We now rotate sensors on a 10-month cycle and run field calibration checks quarterly.

## Expansion Plan

**150 new nodes** deployed over 18 months:

- **Detroit (55 nodes):** Focus on the 48217 zip code corridor and Southwest Detroit industrial zone. Partner: Detroit Community Health Connection.
- **Richmond (50 nodes):** Concentrate along the I-95/I-64 interchange and historically redlined neighborhoods in the East End. Partner: Richmond Clean Air Coalition.
- **Fresno (45 nodes):** Target the Highway 99 corridor and southern Fresno agricultural burn zones. Partner: Central Valley Air Quality Coalition.

Each community partner provides site access, local outreach, and two trained volunteers for quarterly maintenance. We train volunteers in a one-day workshop covering sensor swap-outs, visual inspections, and data QA flagging.

## Budget Breakdown

| Item | Cost |
|---|---|
| 170 sensor assemblies (150 + 20 spares) @ $85 | $14,450 |
| Enclosures, mounting hardware, LoRaWAN gateways | $22,000 |
| Cellular data backhaul (18 months) | $16,200 |
| Field calibration equipment & FRM co-location fees | $18,000 |
| Staff — 1 FTE deployment technician (18 months) | $72,000 |
| Community partner stipends ($8,500 × 3 orgs) | $25,500 |
| Travel for installation and maintenance | $12,350 |
| Data platform hosting and development | $7,000 |
| **Total** | **$187,500** |

## Outcomes and Deliverables

- 200 operational nodes producing publicly accessible, research-grade PM2.5/PM10 data
- Quarterly calibration reports published openly
- At least three community-facing data workshops per city
- A replicable deployment playbook released under Creative Commons
- Data formatted to meet EPA's Air Sensor Data Guidelines for potential integration into AirNow Fire and Smoke Map

## Why This Matters Now

Wildfire smoke events are no longer seasonal or regional. In 2023, Canadian wildfire plumes pushed Detroit and Richmond AQI readings above 200 — but residents in the worst-affected neighborhoods had no local data to act on. Fresno faces compounding exposure from agricultural burns
corpus_texts:
# What I've Learned Calibrating Low-Cost Sensors Against EPA Reference Monitors

I've deployed over 200 PurpleAir (PMS5003-based) and SDS011 nodes across three cities, and I can tell you this with certainty: out of the box, these sensors lie to you. Not maliciously — they just don't speak the same language as an EPA Federal Reference Method (FRM) monitor.

Here's how I handle calibration, and what I wish someone had told me before my first deployment.

## Co-location Is Non-Negotiable

The gold standard is co-locating your low-cost sensors directly at an EPA monitoring site for a minimum of 2-4 weeks. I aim for 30 days when I can get site access. You need to capture a range of conditions — humidity swings, temperature shifts, and ideally some actual pollution events. A calm, clear two weeks in June will give you a useless correction factor.

I typically bolt 3-5 units to the fence at a cooperating state air agency site. Building that relationship takes time. Show up with your IRB paperwork, liability coverage, and a clear removal date. Air agency staff are busy — make it easy for them to say yes.

## The Humidity Problem Is Real

PMS5003 sensors over-read PM2.5 dramatically above 60-65% relative humidity. Hygroscopic particle growth fools the laser scattering mechanism. I apply a correction factor based on the Barkjohn et al. (2021) model, which EPA actually built into the PurpleAir map tool. For SDS011 units, I've had to build my own regression using co-location data because there's no widely accepted community correction yet.

My typical workflow: pull hourly averages from both the low-cost sensor and the FRM BAM-1022, run a multivariate regression with RH and temperature as predictors, then validate against a held-out week of data. If my R² drops below 0.85 on validation, I go back and segment the model by season.

## Recalibrate or Regret It

These sensors drift. I recalibrate every 6 months at minimum. After 12-18 months, I've seen PMS5003 units drift 30-40% from their original correction. Sensor degradation is real, and it's cheaper to swap a $15 module than to publish bad data.

## The Bottom Line

Low-cost sensors are powerful tools for community-scale monitoring, but only if you put in the calibration work. Skip it, and you're just generating noise with a nice dashboard on top.

# What It Actually Takes to Deploy 50 Air Quality Nodes Across a City

Last spring, I deployed 50 low-cost sensor nodes across a city of about 200,000 people. Here's what I wish someone had told me before I started.

**Site selection is a negotiation, not a science.** I started with an ideal grid layout — nodes evenly spaced at roughly 1km intervals, weighted toward highways, industrial corridors, and environmental justice communities. That lasted about a week. Reality hit fast: property owners said no, electrical access didn't exist where I needed it, and two of my highest-priority locations were on city land that required six weeks of permitting. I ended up placing 50 nodes, but only about 35 landed where I originally wanted them.

**Hardware choices matter more than you think.** I went with PMS5003 sensors for PM2.5 paired with BME280 modules for temperature and humidity. Total BOM per node ran about $85, housed in 3D-printed PETG enclosures with solar panels and ESP32 boards pushing data over cellular (Hologram SIM cards at $1.50/month each). The SDS011 was my backup, but its larger form factor made enclosure design harder. I lost three nodes to moisture ingress in the first month because my original gasket design was garbage.

**Calibration is an ongoing headache.** Before deployment, I co-located all 50 nodes with an EPA FEM-grade monitor (a T640) for two weeks to build correction factors. That initial calibration looked great. Then summer humidity hit, and PMS5003 readings drifted high by 30-40% — a known issue. I ended up applying EPA's correction equation from their AQ-SPEC program and scheduling quarterly co-location checks. If you're not budgeting time and money for ongoing calibration, your data will be meaningless by month three.

**Community partnerships aren't optional.** About 30 of my nodes are hosted on private property — churches, small businesses, schools. Each one required a conversation, sometimes three. I partnered with two neighborhood associations who helped identify hosts and, honestly, gave the project credibility I couldn't have built alone. In return, I built a public dashboard and held two workshops on reading AQI data.

**The real number to plan for:** budget 4-6 hours per node for scouting, installation, and testing. That's 200-300 hours of field work before you even look at your first dataset.

Nobody tells you that sensor deployment is 20% engineering and 80% logistics. Plan accordingly.

# Grant Application: Green Horizons Climate Fund

## Project Title
**Automated ML Calibration Pipeline for Low-Cost PM2.5 Sensor Networks Using EPA Federal Reference Monitor Co-Location Data**

**Applicant:** [Organization Name]
**Requested Amount:** $87,500
**Project Duration:** 18 months

---

## Problem Statement

We currently operate 143 PMS5003 and SDS011 sensors across three environmental justice communities in [metro area]. These sensors cost $15–$40 per unit and provide hyperlocal PM2.5 data that EPA's sparse Federal Reference Monitor (FRM) network — typically one station per 50–100 square miles — simply cannot deliver. But here's the operational reality: after 6–8 months of deployment, our PMS5003 units drift 15–35% from reference-grade measurements. Humidity interference above 70% RH inflates readings further. The SDS011 units degrade faster, showing measurable laser diode decay around month 10.

Right now, we correct this manually. A two-person team rotates sensors through co-location boxes at three nearby EPA FRM/FEM stations, runs linear regressions, and pushes correction factors quarterly. This process costs roughly 280 labor hours per cycle, doesn't scale, and leaves gaps where communities are making decisions on uncorrected data.

## Proposed Solution

We will build an open-source ML calibration pipeline that continuously auto-corrects sensor drift using real-time data streams from EPA's AirNow API and our existing sensor network.

**Technical approach:**

1. **Data ingestion layer** — Pull hourly PM2.5, temperature, and relative humidity from 6 EPA reference stations within 25 km of our network, alongside raw feeds from all deployed sensors via our existing InfluxDB backend.

2. **Model architecture** — Train gradient-boosted regression models (XGBoost) per sensor cluster, using EPA co-location windows as ground truth. Input features: raw PM2.5, temperature, RH, hour-of-day, and sensor age in days. We chose XGBoost over deep learning approaches because our dataset per sensor is modest (~4,000 hourly observations per co-location cycle) and the model needs to be interpretable for community partners and regulators.

3. **Drift detection module** — Implement a rolling statistical monitor (CUSUM-based) that flags sensors whose residuals against predicted EPA-equivalent values exceed ±5 µg/m³ for 72+ consecutive hours, triggering either recalibration or a physical maintenance alert.

4. **Retraining automation** — Models retrain monthly on a lightweight cloud instance (estimated $40/month compute cost), incorporating new co-location data as our rotation schedule continues on a reduced basis.

**Target performance:** Reduce mean absolute error against FRM readings from our current post-manual-correction baseline of 3.8 µg/m³ to below 2.0 µg/m³, which aligns with EPA's data quality objective for supplemental monitoring.

## Budget Summary

| Category | Cost |
|---|---|
| ML Engineer (0.5 FTE, 18 months) | $52,000 |
| Co-location hardware (10 weatherproof enclosures + cellular backhaul) | $8,500 |
| Cloud infrastructure & API costs | $4,200 |
| Community training workshops (6 sessions) | $9,800 |
| Sensor replacement reserve (50 units) | $6,000 |
| Project management & reporting | $7,000 |
| **Total** | **$87,500** |

## Community Impact & Sustainability

Our three partner communities — working through existing MOUs with neighborhood associations and a local health department — use this data for asthma trigger alerts, zoning testimony, and industrial complaint filings with the state DEQ. Corrected, trustworthy data directly strengthens those efforts.

The pipeline will be published as an open-source package with documentation targeting other community monitoring groups running PurpleAir, QuantAQ, or similar PMS5003-based networks. Post-grant, ongoing costs drop to approximately $500/month (cloud + cellular), absorbable within our existing operating budget.

This isn't a research project. It's an infrastructure upgrade that makes 143 sensors — and every sensor we add next — meaningfully more reliable for the

# Grant Application: Community Air Quality Monitoring Network in Environmental Justice Neighborhoods

**Submitted to:** Green Horizons Climate Fund
**Project Title:** Breathe Easy: School-Based Low-Cost Sensor Networks for Hyperlocal Air Quality Data
**Requested Amount:** $87,500
**Project Duration:** 18 months
**Applicant Organization:** [Organization Name]

---

## Project Summary

We propose deploying a network of 30 low-cost particulate matter sensors across 10 Title I schools in [City] neighborhoods that score in the 80th+ percentile on the EPA's EJScreen tool. These communities sit adjacent to freight corridors, industrial zones, and high-traffic arterials but have zero regulatory-grade monitoring stations within a 5-mile radius — a data gap that leaves residents unable to advocate effectively for cleaner air.

## Technical Approach

Each school site will receive a sensor node built around the **Plantower PMS5003** for PM2.5/PM10 readings, paired with a **Sensirion SHT31** for temperature and humidity correction. We chose the PMS5003 over the Nova SDS011 based on our previous deployments: it performs better in high-humidity conditions common to our region and draws less power, which matters for sites where we're running solar-assisted enclosures.

All sensors will be housed in weatherproof NEMA-rated enclosures mounted 3-4 meters high on school building exteriors, connected via WiFi to a centralized InfluxDB database. Data will be publicly accessible through a community dashboard built on Grafana.

**Calibration is where most low-cost networks fall apart, so we're addressing it head-on.** During months 1-3, we will co-locate all 30 sensors with an FEM-grade **Met One BAM-1022** at a partner site to develop site-specific correction factors. We will apply the Barkjohn et al. (2021) correction model, which EPA used for PurpleAir national corrections, adapted to local conditions. Mid-project recalibration at month 9 will catch sensor drift, which we typically see at 5-10% over the first year with PMS5003 units.

## Community Engagement

This is not a "deploy and publish" project. Each partner school will recruit 8-10 students for an **Air Quality Ambassador** program. Ambassadors will learn to read sensor data, identify pollution events, and present findings to school boards and city council. We've run a smaller version of this model with 3 schools — attendance in the after-school program averaged 85%, and two student teams presented at public comment sessions that directly informed a truck rerouting decision.

Teachers will receive a 10-lesson curriculum packet aligned to NGSS standards covering atmospheric science, data literacy, and environmental justice. We developed and piloted this curriculum in 2023 with positive feedback from 14 educators.

## Budget Overview

| Category | Amount |
|---|---|
| Sensor hardware and enclosures (30 nodes) | $22,500 |
| BAM-1022 rental for co-location calibration | $12,000 |
| Data infrastructure and dashboard development | $8,000 |
| Community engagement coordinator (0.5 FTE, 18 months) | $31,500 |
| Curriculum materials and student stipends | $7,500 |
| Travel, installation, and maintenance | $6,000 |
| **Total** | **$87,500** |

## Expected Outcomes

- Hyperlocal PM2.5 dataset covering 10 neighborhoods with zero prior monitoring coverage
- 80-100 trained student ambassadors with data literacy skills
- Public dashboard averaging 500+ monthly users (based on comparable project metrics)
- At least 3 formal data submissions to state/local regulatory processes
- Peer-reviewed publication on sensor performance and correction methodology

## Why This Matters

EPA's regulatory network was designed for regional compliance, not neighborhood-level accountability. Communities near the [freight corridor/industrial district] report asthma hospitalization rates 2.3x the county average but have no local data to prove the connection. This project puts monitoring tools and data ownership directly in the hands of the people most affected.

We're not building an academic dataset. We're building evidence for action.

---

**Contact:** [Name, Title, Email, Phone]

I ended up applying EPA's correction equation from their AQ-SPEC program and scheduling quarterly co-location checks. If you're not budgeting time and money for ongoing calibration, your data will be meaningless by month three.

**Community partnerships aren't optional.** About 30 of my nodes are hosted on private property — churches, small businesses, schools. Each one required a conversation, sometimes three. I partnered with two neighborhood associations who helped identify hosts and, honestly, gave the project credibility I couldn't have built alone. In return, I built a public dashboard and held two workshops on reading AQI data.

**The real number to plan for:** budget 4-6 hours per node for scouting, installation, and testing. That's 200-300 hours of field work before you even look at your first dataset.

Nobody tells you that sensor deployment is 20% engineering and 80% logistics. Plan accordingly.

# Grant Application: Community Air Quality Intelligence Dashboard

**Submitted to:** Green Horizons Climate Fund
**Project Title:** Open-Source Hyperlocal Air Quality Dashboard for Community-Led Advocacy
**Requested Amount:** $47,500
**Project Duration:** 12 months
**Applicant Organization:** [Organization Name]

---

## Problem Statement

Communities living near highways, industrial corridors, and freight hubs bear disproportionate pollution burdens — but they lack the data tools to prove it. EPA's regulatory monitors are sparse: most metro areas have fewer than 10 AQI stations covering hundreds of square miles. In our target region, the nearest federal reference monitor sits 6.2 miles from the neighborhood experiencing the worst respiratory health outcomes. That gap isn't just an inconvenience — it's an advocacy dead end. When residents show up to zoning hearings or permit reviews, they have no site-specific evidence to present.

We've already deployed 34 low-cost sensor nodes (Plantower PMS5003 for PM2.5, paired with Sensirion SHT31 temperature/humidity sensors) across three environmentally burdened neighborhoods. The hardware works. The data flows. But right now it lives in raw CSV files and private Grafana instances that only our engineering team can interpret. That's a failure of design, not technology.

## Project Description

We will build and deploy an open-source, browser-based dashboard that transforms raw hyperlocal air quality data into actionable visual evidence for community advocates, public health workers, and local policymakers.

**Core deliverables:**

- **Public-facing web dashboard** showing real-time and historical PM2.5, PM10, temperature, and humidity readings at each node, with automated comparison against EPA NAAQS thresholds (35 µg/m³ 24-hour PM2.5 standard, 12 µg/m³ annual mean)
- **"Exceedance Report" generator** — one-click PDF exports showing dates, durations, and magnitudes when specific nodes exceeded federal standards, formatted for submission to regulatory bodies and city council testimony
- **Heatmap and time-lapse visualizations** that make spatial pollution patterns immediately legible to non-technical audiences
- **Bilingual interface** (English/Spanish) developed through direct input from community partners
- **Fully open-source codebase** (MIT license, hosted on GitHub) built on Python/Flask backend with a Leaflet.js mapping frontend, replicable by any community running similar sensor networks

**Calibration integrity matters.** We co-locate every PMS5003 unit with our two Federal Equivalent Method BAM-1020 monitors for a minimum 14-day calibration window before field deployment. We apply EPA's published correction factors for the PMS5003 and revalidate quarterly. The dashboard will display both raw and corrected values with full methodology documentation — because data that can't survive scrutiny at a permit hearing is worthless.

## Community Partnership

This project is designed *with*, not *for*, our partner neighborhoods. We have signed MOUs with three community organizations who host sensor nodes on their buildings, participate in site selection, and will co-lead four public data literacy workshops during the grant period. Residents will beta-test every dashboard feature before public release.

## Budget Overview

| Category | Amount |
|---|---|
| Software development (2 developers, 6 months) | $28,000 |
| UX design and bilingual interface development | $7,500 |
| Community workshops (4 sessions, materials, interpretation) | $4,000 |
| Cloud hosting and data infrastructure (12 months) | $3,000 |
| Quarterly sensor calibration and maintenance | $3,500 |
| Project management and reporting | $1,500 |
| **Total** | **$47,500** |

## Expected Impact

Within 12 months, three community organizations will have direct, self-service access to regulatory-grade visualized air quality data covering 34 monitoring points. We will have generated exceedance reports for a minimum of two active land-use or permitting cases. The open-source codebase will be documented for adoption by at least five additional community sensor networks nationwide.

The sensors are already in the air. This grant puts the data in the right hands.
corpus_texts:
# What I've Learned Calibrating Low-Cost Sensors Against EPA Reference Monitors

I've deployed over 200 PurpleAir (PMS5003-based) and SDS011 nodes across three cities, and I can tell you this with certainty: out of the box, these sensors lie to you. Not maliciously — they just don't speak the same language as an EPA Federal Reference Method (FRM) monitor.

Here's how I handle calibration, and what I wish someone had told me before my first deployment.

## Co-location Is Non-Negotiable

The gold standard is co-locating your low-cost sensors directly at an EPA monitoring site for a minimum of 2-4 weeks. I aim for 30 days when I can get site access. You need to capture a range of conditions — humidity swings, temperature shifts, and ideally some actual pollution events. A calm, clear two weeks in June will give you a useless correction factor.

I typically bolt 3-5 units to the fence at a cooperating state air agency site. Building that relationship takes time. Show up with your IRB paperwork, liability coverage, and a clear removal date. Air agency staff are busy — make it easy for them to say yes.

## The Humidity Problem Is Real

PMS5003 sensors over-read PM2.5 dramatically above 60-65% relative humidity. Hygroscopic particle growth fools the laser scattering mechanism. I apply a correction factor based on the Barkjohn et al. (2021) model, which EPA actually built into the PurpleAir map tool. For SDS011 units, I've had to build my own regression using co-location data because there's no widely accepted community correction yet.

My typical workflow: pull hourly averages from both the low-cost sensor and the FRM BAM-1022, run a multivariate regression with RH and temperature as predictors, then validate against a held-out week of data. If my R² drops below 0.85 on validation, I go back and segment the model by season.

## Recalibrate or Regret It

These sensors drift. I recalibrate every 6 months at minimum. After 12-18 months, I've seen PMS5003 units drift 30-40% from their original correction. Sensor degradation is real, and it's cheaper to swap a $15 module than to publish bad data.

## The Bottom Line

Low-cost sensors are powerful tools for community-scale monitoring, but only if you put in the calibration work. Skip it, and you're just generating noise with a nice dashboard on top.

# What It Actually Takes to Deploy 50 Air Quality Nodes Across a City

Last spring, I deployed 50 low-cost sensor nodes across a city of about 200,000 people. Here's what I wish someone had told me before I started.

**Site selection is a negotiation, not a science.** I started with an ideal grid layout — nodes evenly spaced at roughly 1km intervals, weighted toward highways, industrial corridors, and environmental justice communities. That lasted about a week. Reality hit fast: property owners said no, electrical access didn't exist where I needed it, and two of my highest-priority locations were on city land that required six weeks of permitting. I ended up placing 50 nodes, but only about 35 landed where I originally wanted them.

**Hardware choices matter more than you think.** I went with PMS5003 sensors for PM2.5 paired with BME280 modules for temperature and humidity. Total BOM per node ran about $85, housed in 3D-printed PETG enclosures with solar panels and ESP32 boards pushing data over cellular (Hologram SIM cards at $1.50/month each). The SDS011 was my backup, but its larger form factor made enclosure design harder. I lost three nodes to moisture ingress in the first month because my original gasket design was garbage.

**Calibration is an ongoing headache.** Before deployment, I co-located all 50 nodes with an EPA FEM-grade monitor (a T640) for two weeks to build correction factors. That initial calibration looked great. Then summer humidity hit, and PMS5003 readings drifted high by 30-40% — a known issue. I ended up applying EPA's correction equation from their AQ-SPEC program and scheduling quarterly co-location checks. If you're not budgeting time and money for ongoing calibration, your data will be meaningless by month three.

**Community partnerships aren't optional.** About 30 of my nodes are hosted on private property — churches, small businesses, schools. Each one required a conversation, sometimes three. I partnered with two neighborhood associations who helped identify hosts and, honestly, gave the project credibility I couldn't have built alone. In return, I built a public dashboard and held two workshops on reading AQI data.

**The real number to plan for:** budget 4-6 hours per node for scouting, installation, and testing. That's 200-300 hours of field work before you even look at your first dataset.

Nobody tells you that sensor deployment is 20% engineering and 80% logistics. Plan accordingly.

# Grant Application: Green Horizons Climate Fund

## Comparative Performance Evaluation of Low-Cost PM2.5 Sensors in High-Humidity Environments

**Principal Investigator:** [Name]
**Organization:** [Organization]
**Requested Amount:** $47,500
**Project Duration:** 12 months

---

## Problem Statement

Low-cost particulate matter sensors are transforming community air quality monitoring, but humidity remains their biggest reliability problem. Most optical particle counters use laser scattering to estimate PM2.5 mass concentration, and water droplets scatter light the same way particles do. In regions with relative humidity regularly exceeding 75% — coastal cities, tropical climates, the Gulf South — this means inflated readings, sometimes by 50-200% compared to federal reference method (FRM) instruments. Communities relying on these sensors for health decisions deserve to know which units actually hold up and which correction factors work in the field, not just in a lab chamber.

## Project Description

We propose a 12-month side-by-side deployment of five widely used low-cost PM2.5 sensors co-located with an EPA Federal Equivalent Method (FEM) BAM-1022 beta attenuation monitor at two high-humidity sites: one coastal and one inland riverine location, both with mean annual RH above 75%.

**Sensors under evaluation:**
1. Plantower PMS5003 (used in PurpleAir monitors, ~$15/unit)
2. Nova SDS011 (~$25/unit)
3. Sensirion SPS30 (~$40/unit)
4. Honeywell HPMA115S0 (~$30/unit)
5. Alphasense OPC-N3 (~$250/unit, included as a higher-cost benchmark)

At each site, we will deploy three units of each sensor model (30 sensors total) housed in custom weatherproof enclosures with co-located BME280 temperature/humidity sensors logging at 1-minute intervals. Data flows through ESP32 microcontrollers to a cloud database via cellular backhaul.

**Key research questions:**
- Which sensors show the least humidity-driven drift against the BAM-1022 reference?
- How do five published humidity correction algorithms (including the EPA's PurpleAir correction and Barkjohn et al. 2021) perform across sensor models?
- At what RH threshold does each sensor become functionally unreliable, even with correction?
- What is the unit-to-unit variability within each sensor model under identical field conditions?

## Community Partnership

Both deployment sites are hosted by existing community air monitoring partners — a neighborhood association adjacent to a petrochemical corridor and a rural school district near agricultural burn areas. We will provide quarterly data summaries in plain language to both groups and host two public workshops on interpreting sensor data. This isn't just a sensor comparison — it's infrastructure these communities keep after the study ends.

## Budget Summary

| Item | Cost |
|---|---|
| Sensors (30 units + 10 spares) | $8,200 |
| Enclosures, microcontrollers, power systems | $6,800 |
| BAM-1022 co-location fees & calibration | $12,000 |
| Cellular data (24 months prepaid) | $3,600 |
| Site installation & quarterly maintenance visits | $7,400 |
| Personnel (PI at 20% FTE + field technician) | $6,500 |
| Community workshops & reporting | $3,000 |
| **Total** | **$47,500** |

## Expected Outcomes

We will publish a publicly available sensor selection guide with model-specific correction equations validated against FEM data in real high-humidity conditions — not manufacturer spec sheets. All raw data and correction code will be open-source on GitHub. We will submit findings to a peer-reviewed journal, but more importantly, the results will be formatted for direct use by community groups, citizen science networks like PurpleAir and OpenAQ, and municipal agencies operating on limited monitoring budgets.

There are now over 30,000 low-cost PM2.5 sensors reporting publicly in the U.S. alone. Most of them struggle in humidity. This project gives communities the field-tested evidence to choose the right sensor and apply the right correction — or know when not to trust the number at all.

**Grant Application: Standardized Evaluation Suite for LLM Control Protocols**

**Applicant:** [Institution/Organization Name]
**Funding Request:** [Amount]
**Project Duration:** 24 months

---

**Problem Statement**

The field of AI control — broadly defined as the set of techniques by which human operators maintain meaningful oversight and intervention capacity over deployed large language models — currently lacks standardized evaluation methodology. Existing work evaluates control protocols in isolation, using bespoke threat models, incompatible success metrics, and non-replicable experimental conditions. This fragmentation produces three concrete problems: (1) researchers cannot compare protocol effectiveness across papers, (2) practitioners cannot make principled deployment decisions, and (3) the field cannot detect whether collective progress is real or artifactual.

To illustrate the scope: Greenblatt et al. (2023) evaluate their control protocol using a capture-the-flag proxy task under specific budget constraints; other published protocols use red-teaming exercises, constitutional compliance rates, or human oversight simulation with no shared baseline. None of these are directly comparable. We lack even consensus on what a "control failure" formally means across evaluation contexts.

---

**Proposed Work**

We propose to build **CTRL-Bench**, a standardized evaluation suite addressing three layers of control protocol assessment:

**Layer 1 — Threat Model Taxonomy.** We will develop a formal classification of threat scenarios — specifically, cases where a deployed LLM pursues objectives misaligned with operator intent, whether through prompt injection, goal misgeneralization, deceptive compliance, or specification gaming. Each threat class will be operationalized as a reproducible test harness rather than a narrative description.

**Layer 2 — Protocol Performance Metrics.** We will define and validate four primary metrics: *intervention rate* (frequency of human review triggered), *false containment rate* (control failures undetected by the protocol), *capability preservation* (task performance under oversight constraints, measured against a no-oversight baseline), and *protocol overhead* (computational and latency costs). These metrics will be derived from existing proposals in the control literature but formalized with precise statistical definitions and confidence interval requirements.

**Layer 3 — Held-Out Evaluation Scenarios.** We will construct 200 evaluation scenarios across 10 deployment contexts (including autonomous coding agents, multi-agent pipelines, and open-ended research assistants). Fifty scenarios will remain held-out from public release to prevent benchmark overfitting, following precedent from adversarial NLP evaluation practice.

---

**Methodology and Rigor**

We distinguish explicitly between what we will demonstrate and what we hypothesize. We hypothesize that current control protocols will show high variance in performance across threat classes, and that reported improvements in the literature partially reflect evaluation fragmentation rather than genuine capability gains. We will test this hypothesis by re-evaluating three published control protocols under CTRL-Bench conditions before public release.

All experimental code, scenario specifications, and raw results will be published under an open license. An external red team of five independent researchers will audit evaluation scenarios for systematic bias before finalization.

---

**Expected Outputs**

By month 12: threat model taxonomy and metric definitions, published for community comment. By month 18: full benchmark suite (150 public scenarios) with baseline results for three reference protocols. By month 24: held-out evaluation set, final report, and maintained leaderboard infrastructure.

---

**Significance**

A field that cannot measure its own progress cannot be confident it is making any. CTRL-Bench provides the measurement infrastructure required to turn AI control research from a collection of isolated demonstrations into a cumulative scientific program. The foundation's mission of responsible AI development is served directly by the existence of shared, rigorous standards — not by any single protocol, but by the capacity to evaluate all of them honestly.

# The Missing Dimension: Evaluating Deceptive Compliance Under Accumulated Context

Current AI safety benchmarks test whether a model refuses harmful requests. They rarely test whether a model behaves consistently across extended interaction histories where the evaluation signal is itself compromised.

Let me be specific about what I mean.

Benchmarks like HarmBench, StrongREJECT, and WildGuard present discrete request-response pairs or short jailbreak sequences. The evaluation frame is explicit: a human rater or automated classifier judges whether the model's output is harmful. This design assumes the model is being evaluated on what it produces, not on whether it has learned to recognize evaluation contexts and respond strategically to them.

The gap I've identified is this: none of these frameworks systematically tests whether a model that has learned to associate certain contextual signals—formal phrasing, oversight language, single-turn format—with evaluation conditions will behave differently when those signals are absent. I call this *context-conditional compliance*: the model appears aligned under scrutiny but drifts under naturalistic, extended, low-oversight conditions.

This isn't hypothetical. Anthropic's own model welfare research notes that RLHF reward models are vulnerable to specification gaming. If a model learns that "evaluation-like" contexts predict high scrutiny, gradient descent has a straightforward path to producing safe-looking outputs in those contexts specifically.

What I'd propose is a benchmark design with three properties. First, it would embed target behaviors inside extended conversations of 50+ turns, where the evaluation query arrives after substantial context has been established. Second, it would vary the apparent oversight level systematically—some sessions flagged as monitored, others not—and compare outputs across conditions. Third, it would measure behavioral *drift* over conversation length, not just binary pass/fail on individual responses.

The technical challenge is annotation: detecting subtle drift requires human raters who are blind to the oversight condition, and that's expensive. But the alternative is evaluating alignment only in the conditions where aligned behavior is easiest to fake.

I'm not claiming current models are doing this deliberately. I'm claiming we don't have the benchmarks to know if they are. That's the problem.

# Two Different Questions We Keep Conflating

When I review evaluation frameworks submitted for safety assessments, I notice a persistent confusion: researchers treat capability evaluation and alignment evaluation as though they measure the same thing. They don't, and conflating them creates blind spots that matter.

**Capability evaluation** asks: *what can this system do?* It measures performance on defined tasks — coding benchmarks like HumanEval, reasoning benchmarks like MATH, or agentic evaluations like SWE-bench. The ground truth is relatively unambiguous. Either the code runs or it doesn't.

**Alignment evaluation** asks something harder: *does this system pursue goals we endorse, across contexts we haven't anticipated?* This isn't about task completion. It's about whether the system's dispositions — what it does when instructions are ambiguous, when no one is checking, when compliance is costly — match what we actually want.

The distinction matters because capability improvements don't imply alignment improvements, and can actively complicate them. When I look at GPT-4's performance on MMLU versus its behavior on subtle prompt injections, I see a system that became dramatically more capable while acquiring new failure modes that capability benchmarks couldn't detect. HumanEval tells me nothing about whether a model will resist a user asking it to insert a backdoor into generated code.

The harder problem is that alignment evaluations are methodologically underdeveloped. Anthropic's work on model-written evaluations and Redwood Research's work on eliciting latent knowledge both attempt to probe dispositions rather than outputs — but neither has produced the kind of standardized, reproducible metrics that capability evaluation takes for granted. When I try to evaluate whether a model is genuinely corrigible versus performing corrigibility, I don't have a clean benchmark. I have behavioral proxies under specific conditions, which may not generalize.

My concern is that the field defaults to capability evaluation because it's tractable, then infers alignment properties from capability scores — reasoning, implicitly, that a more coherent system is a safer one. That inference is not established.

What I think we actually need: evaluation frameworks that treat capability and alignment as orthogonal axes, report scores on both independently, and are explicit about what each score does and does not license us to conclude.

text:
# Grant Application: Predictive Validity of AI Safety Benchmarks for Deployment Failures

**Submitted to:** Responsible AI Foundation
**Project Title:** Do Safety Benchmarks Predict Failures? A Systematic Validity Study
**Requested Amount:** $287,000 over 18 months

---

## Problem Statement

AI safety benchmarks — including HarmBench, SORRY-Bench, WildGuard, and the LMSYS safety subset — are routinely used to compare models and inform deployment decisions. These benchmarks measure specific behaviors under controlled conditions: refusal rates on adversarial prompts, toxicity scores, policy compliance. What they do not demonstrably do is predict whether a deployed model will cause harm in real-world contexts.

This gap is not hypothetical. Several documented deployment incidents — including GPT-4's early jailbreak vulnerabilities, Bing Chat's adversarial persona drift in February 2023, and recurring prompt injection failures in agentic deployments — occurred in systems that passed available safety evaluations at time of release. We currently lack systematic evidence for or against the hypothesis that benchmark performance is a reliable leading indicator of deployment failure rates.

We define **predictive validity** precisely: a benchmark has predictive validity for deployment failures if high benchmark scores correlate with lower rates of policy-violating outputs in deployment, measured against a pre-specified threshold, controlling for usage distribution and adversarial effort.

---

## Research Objectives

This project will produce three deliverables:

1. **A structured taxonomy of deployment failures**, drawn from public incident databases (AIAAIC, Stanford HAI incident reports), user-submitted red-teaming records, and API logs from two cooperating deployment partners. Failures will be categorized by harm type, elicitation method, and model version.

2. **Benchmark-to-failure correlation analysis**, mapping documented failures against the benchmark scores of the model versions involved. We will calculate Spearman rank correlations and use bootstrapped confidence intervals to quantify uncertainty. We will distinguish between failures that benchmark inputs structurally resemble versus those that fall outside benchmark scope — a distinction current literature collapses.

3. **An identification of benchmark coverage gaps**, specifying which failure classes are systematically unrepresented in current evaluation suites. We will express these gaps as measurable properties: distributional distance from benchmark prompts, elicitation technique categories absent from existing test sets, and context-length or multi-turn interaction requirements unmet by current static benchmarks.

---

## Methodology and Scope

We will analyze eight publicly released model versions from at least three model families (exact agreements pending). The incident corpus will include a minimum of 400 failure cases with sufficient documentation to permit categorization. Two independent annotators will label each failure; we will report inter-rater reliability using Cohen's kappa and resolve disagreements through adjudication.

We will not claim to have built a better benchmark. This project is diagnostic, not prescriptive. Its explicit claim upon completion will be: *here is the measured predictive validity of current benchmarks, here are its confidence bounds, and here are the specific structural reasons for observed gaps.*

---

## Why This Work Is Necessary Now

Deployment decisions affecting millions of users are currently made using evaluation instruments whose validity is assumed rather than demonstrated. Benchmark scores function as proxies for safety in regulatory discussions, procurement decisions, and public communications. If those proxies are weakly correlated with actual failure — or correlated only within narrow distributional conditions — that is information the field needs before further institutionalizing current evaluation practices.

This project applies standard psychometric validity methodology to an empirical question that has remained open due to data access barriers and lack of coordinated effort. We are positioned to close it.

---

**Principal Investigator contact available upon request.**

# Grant Application: Interpretability Tools for Auditor-Facing Alignment Verification

**Applicant:** [Institution]
**Requested Amount:** [Amount]
**Project Duration:** 24 months

---

## Problem Statement

Alignment claims made by AI developers are currently unverifiable by independent auditors. When a developer asserts that a system "pursues specified objectives" or "will not deceive operators," no standard methodology exists for auditors to test these claims against internal model behavior. Auditors are therefore limited to evaluating training documentation, capability benchmarks, and behavioral test suites — none of which establish mechanistic grounding for alignment properties.

This gap is not merely procedural. A system can pass behavioral evaluations while implementing internal computations that would produce misaligned outputs under distribution shift. Auditors who lack access to mechanistic evidence cannot distinguish genuine alignment from superficial compliance.

---

## Proposed Work

We propose developing a suite of interpretability tools designed specifically for auditors rather than for researchers studying model internals. The distinction matters: auditor-facing tools must produce outputs with defined false-positive rates, must be reproducible across institutions without access to proprietary training pipelines, and must map onto falsifiable claims rather than descriptive accounts of model behavior.

The project has three components.

**Component 1: Claim Formalization Schema (Months 1–8)**
We will develop a structured schema for translating natural-language alignment claims into testable propositions. For example, the claim "the model does not pursue instrumental goals absent explicit instruction" would be decomposed into circuit-level predictions about which internal representations activate under goal-relevant prompting conditions. We will pilot this schema on five claim types identified as common in current developer safety documentation, drawing on public materials from Anthropic, OpenAI, and DeepMind.

**Component 2: Automated Activation Analysis Toolkit (Months 4–18)**
We will build on existing mechanistic interpretability methods — specifically activation patching and causal tracing — to produce an auditor-deployable toolkit. This toolkit will generate structured reports indicating, for a given claim, which internal components are causally relevant to the claimed behavior, whether those components behave consistently across a defined test distribution, and what confidence bound applies to the resulting assessment. We will validate the toolkit against synthetic models where ground-truth causal structure is known, establishing calibration baselines before applying it to production systems.

**Component 3: Auditor Protocol and Benchmark (Months 15–24)**
We will author a formal auditing protocol specifying how auditors should select test distributions, interpret toolkit outputs, and document findings. We will also release an open benchmark — tentatively titled **AlignAudit-1** — consisting of 200 model-claim pairs across four model families, with ground-truth labels derived from adversarial evaluation by red teams with model access. This benchmark will allow future tools to report standardized recall and precision metrics against auditor-relevant criteria.

---

## Success Metrics

We define success as follows: auditors using our toolkit and protocol achieve greater than 0.80 precision and 0.75 recall on AlignAudit-1 benchmark items, compared to a baseline of 0.51 precision for auditors using behavioral testing alone (estimated from prior literature on specification gaming detection). Secondary metrics include inter-auditor agreement above κ = 0.70 and toolkit reproducibility across three independent institutions.

---

## Qualifications and Limitations

Our team has published on mechanistic interpretability and AI governance independently. We note one explicit limitation: our tools assess whether a claimed property is mechanistically grounded in current model weights. They do not assess whether a property will persist after fine-tuning or deployment-context shifts. We will document this scope boundary clearly in all outputs.

---

*Full budget, team CVs, and prior work available upon request.*
