We propose implementation of advanced sequencing data processing protocols, variant analysis frameworks, and genomic annotation strategies. Our methodology includes application of statistical genetics approaches, network analysis techniques, and systems biology modeling. We will integrate multi-omics datasets using correlation analysis, pathway analysis tools, and biological network construction methods to identify regulatory relationships and potential therapeutic targets.

**Aim 4: Drug Discovery Pipeline Development**
The final objective involves creating an integrated drug discovery workflow combining computational screening, molecular docking, pharmacophore modeling, and ADMET prediction methodologies. We will utilize high-throughput virtual screening approaches alongside machine learning models trained on comprehensive chemical and biological datasets.

### Methodology

Our research design incorporates several complementary technical approaches. We will employ molecular docking simulations, utilizing energy-based scoring functions and conformational sampling algorithms. Phylogenetic analysis will be conducted using maximum likelihood estimation and Bayesian inference methods. Network pharmacology approaches will integrate protein-protein interaction data, gene regulatory networks, and chemical similarity metrics.

Data analysis will incorporate dimensionality reduction techniques, clustering algorithms, supervised and unsupervised machine learning models, and statistical hypothesis testing. We plan to implement both traditional statistical approaches and contemporary deep learning architectures for pattern recognition and predictive modeling.

### Preliminary Results

Our preliminary investigations have generated encouraging findings across all proposed aims. Initial computational studies have identified promising candidates through our integrated analytical approach. Preliminary validation studies suggest the viability of our proposed methodologies, with results indicating substantial promise for further development.

### Expected Outcomes

This project will produce a comprehensive computational platform applicable to multiple stages of drug discovery and development. We anticipate generation of novel insights into protein function, identification of candidate therapeutic targets, and development of predictive models with significant translational potential.

### Impact and Significance

The successful completion of this research will substantially advance computational approaches in biomolecular science, potentially accelerating therapeutic discovery timelines and improving research efficiency across multiple disciplines.

# Grant Application: Deep Science Ventures — Planetary Science Initiative

## Project Title: Subsurface Biosignature Detection via Adversarial Neural Networks Applied to Martian Regolith Spectral Data

## Principal Investigator: Dr. Elena Vasquez-Okafor, Planetary Geochemistry Lab, Utrecht Institute for Earth and Space Sciences

---

## Summary

Mars holds secrets beneath its surface. Our project leverages generative adversarial networks to identify cryptic biosignatures in near-infrared spectral datasets collected from Martian regolith analogue sites across the Atacama Desert and Svalbard permafrost regions. The data remains underutilized. By training discriminator networks on synthetic spectral profiles of known extremophile metabolic byproducts, we can dramatically lower detection thresholds for organic compounds obscured by perchlorate interference and iron oxide spectral masking in current analytical pipelines.

## Problem Statement

Current biosignature detection methods fail systematically. The spectral signatures of ancient microbial life in oxidizing, radiation-bombarded regolith are extraordinarily faint, typically falling two to three orders of magnitude below conventional instrument noise floors on both rover-mounted and orbital spectrometers. Existing algorithms miss subtle patterns. Our preliminary analysis of 14,200 spectra from the ExoMars Trace Gas Orbiter NOMAD instrument suggests that approximately thirty-seven percent of dismissed anomalous readings may contain overlapping absorption features consistent with fossilized lipid membrane degradation products.

## Proposed Approach

We will build open-source adversarial networks. Specifically, our three-phase methodology begins with generating 500,000 synthetic spectral profiles using our proprietary thermodynamic degradation model, BIODECAY-3, which simulates how twenty-six candidate biosignature molecules transform under Martian surface conditions over geological timescales. Phase two involves rigorous training. The discriminator network will learn to distinguish genuine biosignature-bearing spectra from abiotic mineral combinations across a parameter space encompassing temperature, pressure, UV flux, and regolith mineralogy variations documented at Jezero Crater and Oxia Planum. Phase three validates everything. We will deploy our trained model against a blind dataset of 8,400 spectra curated independently by collaborators at the Instituto Nacional de Técnica Aeroespacial in Madrid, who have maintained strict separation protocols throughout.

## Open Science Commitment

All code ships on GitHub immediately. Our BIODECAY-3 degradation model, training datasets, network architectures, and validation protocols will be released under permissive Apache 2.0 licensing, enabling any planetary science group worldwide to reproduce, critique, and extend our analytical framework without restriction. Transparency drives better science. We have already partnered with the Planetary Data System to ensure permanent archival and established a community review board comprising eleven researchers from eight institutions across four continents.

## Budget Overview

We request €287,000 over eighteen months. Personnel costs account for €168,000, covering one postdoctoral researcher specializing in deep learning architectures and one doctoral student with expertise in vibrational spectroscopy of mineral-organic systems. Computing infrastructure requires €74,000. Cloud GPU allocation for training runs, validated through our existing partnership with the Dutch National Supercomputing Centre, constitutes the primary computational expense alongside dedicated storage for our growing spectral database. Remaining funds support collaboration. Travel for cross-validation workshops, open-access publication fees, and community engagement activities comprise the final €45,000.

## Expected Impact

This work could reshape Mars exploration priorities. If our adversarial network approach achieves even a fifty-percent improvement in biosignature detection sensitivity, it would fundamentally alter target selection criteria for ESA's Rosalind Franklin rover and future NASA sample return mission planning. The implications extend beyond Mars. Our open-source framework generalizes naturally to spectral analysis of Enceladus plume data, Europa surface ice observations, and Titan atmospheric chemistry measurements currently being planned for the Dragonfly mission era.

We stand at an extraordinary inflection point in astrobiology. This project ensures artificial intelligence serves as humanity's sharpest lens for finding life beyond Earth.

# The Mysterious Storms of Jupiter

Jupiter's storms are legendary. Among all the planets in our solar system, this gas giant hosts the most violent and long-lasting atmospheric phenomena ever observed by human instruments. The Great Red Spot dominates everything. This anticyclonic storm has been raging for at least three centuries, maintaining a diameter so vast that Earth could fit inside it with room to spare.

Wind speeds there are terrifying. Scientists using data from the Juno spacecraft have measured sustained winds exceeding four hundred miles per hour at certain altitudes within Jupiter's swirling cloud bands. These aren't ordinary weather patterns. The energy driving these massive storms comes primarily from Jupiter's intense internal heat rather than from solar radiation, which makes them fundamentally different from hurricanes on Earth.

New storms appear constantly. Researchers recently discovered that smaller white oval storms can merge together over decades, gradually combining their rotational energy to form increasingly powerful and persistent atmospheric vortices. Color changes reveal hidden chemistry. When a storm dredges up compounds like ammonium hydrosulfide and phosphorus from deeper atmospheric layers, the usually white clouds transform into striking shades of red and brown.

Jupiter has no solid surface. This means that storms face none of the friction-based dissipation that weakens terrestrial hurricanes once they make landfall, allowing Jovian tempests to persist for extraordinary timescales. Lightning strikes illuminate the depths. The Juno mission detected shallow lightning in Jupiter's upper atmosphere, challenging previous assumptions that electrical discharges only occurred deep within the planet's thick ammonia-water cloud layers.

The implications extend beyond Jupiter. Understanding how storms behave on gas giants helps planetary scientists develop better atmospheric models that could eventually be applied to studying exoplanet weather systems. Saturn shows similar patterns too. Both gas giants demonstrate that large, rapidly rotating planets with deep atmospheres naturally generate banded wind structures and persistent vortex systems through mechanisms still being actively investigated.

Planetary storms humble us profoundly. They remind us that Earth's most devastating hurricanes are gentle breezes compared to the colossal, centuries-old maelstroms churning through the atmospheres of our neighboring worlds.

# The Hidden Oceans Beneath Our Feet

Europa captivates planetary scientists worldwide. Beneath its cracked and frozen surface lies a vast liquid water ocean that may contain more water than all of Earth's oceans combined. The ice shell shifts constantly. These tidal flexures, driven by Jupiter's immense gravitational pull, generate enough internal heat to keep that buried ocean in a perpetually liquid state despite frigid surface temperatures.

Enceladus tells a similar story. Saturn's tiny moon shoots spectacular geysers of water vapor and ice particles through fissures near its south pole, confirming an active subsurface ocean exists beneath the crust. Scientists call these ocean worlds. The discovery that liquid water can persist far beyond the traditional habitable zone has fundamentally reshaped our understanding of where life might potentially emerge and thrive.

## Why Subsurface Oceans Matter

Chemistry drives the excitement here. These hidden oceans likely interact with rocky seafloors through hydrothermal processes remarkably similar to those found at mid-ocean ridges deep beneath Earth's own vast oceans. Life needs energy and nutrients. On our planet, thriving ecosystems of tube worms, extremophilic bacteria, and ghostly white crabs cluster around volcanic vents completely independent of sunlight as an energy source.

Could similar ecosystems exist elsewhere? NASA's upcoming Europa Clipper mission will perform dozens of close flybys to measure ice thickness, map surface composition, and search for signs of recent geological activity. The stakes feel enormous. If we detect organic molecules or complex chemistry in the plumes or surface deposits of these distant moons, it would represent perhaps the most profound scientific discovery ever made.

## The Bigger Picture

Titan adds another dimension entirely. Its surface features lakes and rivers of liquid methane and ethane, while a water ocean may lurk deep beneath its thick organic-rich crust, creating two potentially habitable environments stacked vertically. Ocean worlds outnumber dry ones. Current estimates suggest that subsurface oceans could exist on dozens of bodies throughout our solar system, from dwarf planets like Ceres to distant Kuiper Belt objects.

Planetary science stands at a threshold. The next two decades of robotic exploration will determine whether life is a cosmic fluke unique to Earth or an inevitable consequence of chemistry meeting liquid water wherever it persists.

# Grant Application: Subsurface Ocean Chemotype Mapping via Autonomous Spectral Intelligence

## Principal Investigator: Dr. Elara Vasquez-Okonjo, Planetary Geochemistry Laboratory, Utrecht Institute for Exoplanetary Sciences

---

## Project Summary

Europa's ocean hides extraordinary chemical secrets. Our project will deploy a novel machine learning framework called TRIDENT to classify subsurface ocean chemotypes using orbital spectral data collected from both existing and forthcoming planetary missions. This changes everything for astrobiology. By training our models on seventeen years of laboratory analog spectra generated at our cryogenic simulation facility, we can identify biosignature-adjacent molecular assemblages that traditional spectral analysis consistently overlooks.

## The Problem

Current detection methods are painfully limited. Conventional approaches to analyzing ice shell spectral returns rely on rigid band-matching algorithms that cannot account for the nonlinear mixing behaviors characteristic of briny, irradiated cryogenic surfaces under varying tidal stress conditions. We miss critical signals constantly. Our preliminary work on Galileo NIMS reprocessed data has already revealed fourteen previously unidentified spectral features in the trailing hemisphere chaos terrain regions of Europa.

## Technical Approach

TRIDENT uses transformer-based spectral embeddings. Specifically, our architecture ingests hyperspectral radiance cubes and produces probabilistic chemotype maps that encode both compositional identity and confidence intervals across spatial domains with unprecedented granular resolution. The training pipeline is entirely novel. We have constructed a library of over forty-three thousand cryogenic brine spectra using our custom vacuum chamber facility, which operates at temperatures between forty and one hundred thirty Kelvin under jovian radiation-equivalent flux conditions.

Phase one involves model training. Phase two applies the trained TRIDENT framework to reprocessed Galileo and Juno datasets, generating the first comprehensive probabilistic chemotype atlas of Europa's accessible surface composition. Phase three prepares for JUICE. We will optimize our pipeline specifically for the MAJIS instrument aboard ESA's Jupiter Icy Moons Explorer, ensuring that our open-source toolkit is mission-ready before orbital insertion in twenty thirty-one.

## Open Science Commitment

All data products will be immediately public. Our chemotype atlas, trained model weights, spectral library, and analysis pipeline will be released under Creative Commons and MIT licenses through our partnership with the European Planetary Science Archive. Transparency drives better science. We have already established a community advisory board comprising researchers from twelve institutions across nine countries who will guide our data governance and reproducibility protocols.

## Why This Matters

Life needs chemistry and energy. If TRIDENT successfully identifies magnesium sulfate enrichment patterns co-located with endogenic thermal signatures, we will have produced the strongest orbital evidence yet for localized habitable niches within Europa's ice shell fracture network. This directly informs landing site selection. NASA's Europa Lander mission concept specifically identifies surface composition mapping as a critical precursor measurement needed before any life-detection payload can be responsibly deployed.

## Budget Summary

We request €487,000 over thirty months. Personnel costs constitute sixty-two percent of this budget, covering two postdoctoral researchers specializing in deep learning architectures and cryogenic geochemistry, plus one dedicated open-science data engineer. Laboratory operations require nineteen percent. The remaining funds support computational infrastructure, community workshops, and open-access publication fees necessary for maintaining our commitment to fully transparent, reproducible planetary science.

## Team Expertise

Our group pioneered cryogenic analog spectroscopy. Dr. Vasquez-Okonjo has published thirty-seven papers on icy satellite surface chemistry, holds two ESA instrument co-investigatorships, and led the computational framework behind the Ganymede Surface Atlas released in twenty twenty-three. Results speak clearly for themselves.

---

*We believe TRIDENT represents a paradigm shift in how orbital data informs astrobiological exploration, and Deep Science Ventures is uniquely positioned to catalyze this transformation.*

# Grant Application: Autonomous Spectral Classification of Subsurface Volatiles on Ocean Worlds

## Principal Investigator: Dr. Elara Vasquez-Okonkwo, Planetary Geochemistry Lab, Nordic Institute for Astrobiology

---

## Project Summary

Ocean worlds harbor astrobiological secrets. Our project develops an open-source machine learning pipeline that autonomously classifies subsurface volatile signatures from orbital and in-situ spectral data collected across icy moon environments. We call it PRISM-V. This framework could transform how we detect habitable niches beneath kilometers of ice on Europa, Enceladus, and potentially Titan's subsurface ocean.

## The Problem

Current spectral analysis methods are slow. Researchers spend an average of fourteen months manually processing volatile signature datasets from a single flyby mission, creating bottlenecks that delay critical astrobiological interpretations by years. No unified computational framework exists. The planetary science community lacks a standardized, reproducible tool for cross-comparing volatile plume compositions between different ocean worlds using consistent spectral deconvolution methods.

## Our Approach

PRISM-V uses transformer-based architectures. Specifically, we adapt recent advances in self-supervised spectral representation learning to build models that generalize across multiple instrument types, wavelength ranges, and planetary surface conditions without requiring extensive labeled training data. We train on synthetic spectra first. Our collaborators at the Reykjavik Computational Astrophysics Centre have generated over twelve million synthetic near-infrared and ultraviolet spectral profiles simulating volatile mixtures under varying temperature, pressure, and radiation conditions.

The model then fine-tunes on real data. We leverage publicly available datasets from Cassini VIMS, Galileo NIMS, and preliminary Europa Clipper calibration spectra to validate classification accuracy against known volatile compositions. Early benchmarks are promising. In blind tests using Cassini Enceladus plume data, our prototype achieved ninety-three percent accuracy in identifying hydrogen, methane, ammonia, and complex organics within mixed spectral signatures.

## Open Science Commitment

All code lives on public repositories. We will release PRISM-V under a permissive open-source license, with comprehensive documentation, tutorials, and containerized deployment options so any research group worldwide can reproduce and extend our work. Data pipelines will be transparent. Every preprocessing step, model architecture decision, and hyperparameter choice will be logged using standardized provenance tracking that meets FAIR data principles.

## Budget and Timeline

We request £285,000 over twenty-four months. Phase one allocates seven months and £95,000 for synthetic data generation, model architecture development, and recruitment of two postdoctoral computational scientists with expertise in spectroscopy and deep learning. Phase two spans ten months. This period covers real-data validation, cross-instrument generalization testing, and collaboration with three international partner labs in Tokyo, Pasadena, and Gothenburg. Phase three requires seven months. Final resources support community workshops, documentation sprints, open-access publication of results, and integration testing with upcoming JUICE mission spectral formats.

## Why This Matters Now

Europa Clipper launches soon. The JUICE mission is already en route to Jupiter, and within five years the planetary science community will face an unprecedented influx of spectral data from multiple ocean world targets simultaneously. Without autonomous classification tools, we will drown in data while missing biosignature candidates hidden in complex volatile mixtures that human analysts simply cannot process at the required scale and speed.

PRISM-V bridges that gap directly. It represents a fundamentally new capability for the astrobiology community — democratized, reproducible, and built for the data-rich era of ocean world exploration that begins this decade.

## Closing Statement

The search for life demands better tools. Deep Science Ventures' commitment to transformative planetary science makes this partnership a natural and urgent fit for accelerating humanity's greatest scientific question.

# The Mysterious Storms of Jupiter

Jupiter's storms are legendary. Among all the planets in our solar system, this gas giant hosts the most violent and long-lasting atmospheric phenomena ever observed by human instruments. The Great Red Spot dominates everything. This anticyclonic storm has been raging for at least three centuries, maintaining a diameter so vast that Earth could fit inside it with room to spare.

Wind speeds there are terrifying. Scientists using data from the Juno spacecraft have measured sustained winds exceeding four hundred miles per hour at certain altitudes within Jupiter's swirling cloud bands. These aren't ordinary weather patterns. The energy driving these massive storms comes primarily from Jupiter's intense internal heat rather than from solar radiation, which makes them fundamentally different from hurricanes on Earth.

New storms appear constantly. Researchers recently discovered that smaller white oval storms can merge together over decades, gradually combining their rotational energy to form increasingly powerful and persistent atmospheric vortices. Color changes reveal hidden chemistry. When a storm dredges up compounds like ammonium hydrosulfide and phosphorus from deeper atmospheric layers, the usually white clouds transform into striking shades of red and brown.

Jupiter has no solid surface. This means that storms face none of the friction-based dissipation that weakens terrestrial hurricanes once they make landfall, allowing Jovian tempests to persist for extraordinary timescales. Lightning strikes illuminate the depths. The Juno mission detected shallow lightning in Jupiter's upper atmosphere, challenging previous assumptions that electrical discharges only occurred deep within the planet's thick ammonia-water cloud layers.

The implications extend beyond Jupiter. Understanding how storms behave on gas giants helps planetary scientists develop better atmospheric models that could eventually be applied to studying exoplanet weather systems. Saturn shows similar patterns too. Both gas giants demonstrate that large, rapidly rotating planets with deep atmospheres naturally generate banded wind structures and persistent vortex systems through mechanisms still being actively investigated.

Planetary storms humble us profoundly. They remind us that Earth's most devastating hurricanes are gentle breezes compared to the colossal, centuries-old maelstroms churning through the atmospheres of our neighboring worlds.

# The Hidden Oceans Beneath Our Feet

Europa captivates planetary scientists worldwide. Beneath its cracked and frozen surface lies a vast liquid water ocean that may contain more water than all of Earth's oceans combined. The ice shell shifts constantly. These tidal flexures, driven by Jupiter's immense gravitational pull, generate enough internal heat to keep that buried ocean in a perpetually liquid state despite frigid surface temperatures.

Enceladus tells a similar story. Saturn's tiny moon shoots spectacular geysers of water vapor and ice particles through fissures near its south pole, confirming an active subsurface ocean exists beneath the crust. Scientists call these ocean worlds. The discovery that liquid water can persist far beyond the traditional habitable zone has fundamentally reshaped our understanding of where life might potentially emerge and thrive.

## Why Subsurface Oceans Matter

Chemistry drives the excitement here. These hidden oceans likely interact with rocky seafloors through hydrothermal processes remarkably similar to those found at mid-ocean ridges deep beneath Earth's own vast oceans. Life needs energy and nutrients. On our planet, thriving ecosystems of tube worms, extremophilic bacteria, and ghostly white crabs cluster around volcanic vents completely independent of sunlight as an energy source.

Could similar ecosystems exist elsewhere? NASA's upcoming Europa Clipper mission will perform dozens of close flybys to measure ice thickness, map surface composition, and search for signs of recent geological activity. The stakes feel enormous. If we detect organic molecules or complex chemistry in the plumes or surface deposits of these distant moons, it would represent perhaps the most profound scientific discovery ever made.

## The Bigger Picture

Titan adds another dimension entirely. Its surface features lakes and rivers of liquid methane and ethane, while a water ocean may lurk deep beneath its thick organic-rich crust, creating two potentially habitable environments stacked vertically. Ocean worlds outnumber dry ones. Current estimates suggest that subsurface oceans could exist on dozens of bodies throughout our solar system, from dwarf planets like Ceres to distant Kuiper Belt objects.

Planetary science stands at a threshold. The next two decades of robotic exploration will determine whether life is a cosmic fluke unique to Earth or an inevitable consequence of chemistry meeting liquid water wherever it persists.

# Grant Application: Deep Science Ventures — Planetary Science Division

## Project Title: *Spectral Fingerprinting of Subsurface Biosignatures Using Adversarial Neural Networks on Mars Analog Terrains*

**Principal Investigator:** Dr. Cassandra Veltheim, Planetary Geochemistry Lab, Utrecht Institute for Earth and Space Sciences

## Summary

Life leaves chemical traces underground. Our project develops adversarial neural network architectures trained on hyperspectral data from seventeen Mars analog sites across three continents to detect subtle mineralogical patterns consistent with subsurface biological activity. We call this approach FABLE: Fingerprint Analysis of Biosignature Layers in Exoplanetary contexts. No existing method achieves this resolution.

## Problem Statement

Current biosignature detection relies on surface-level spectroscopy. This approach fundamentally misses the most promising habitable zones on Mars, which exist between two and forty meters below the regolith where liquid brines may persist seasonally and shield organic compounds from ultraviolet degradation. The scientific community lacks automated tools. Specifically, we need systems capable of distinguishing biogenic mineral assemblages from purely abiotic formations when working with noisy, incomplete spectral datasets typical of robotic planetary exploration missions.

## Technical Approach

Our method combines three innovations. First, we have assembled a novel hyperspectral dataset comprising 4.2 million labeled spectral measurements from analog sites including the Atacama Desert, Svalbard permafrost cores, and the Pilbara Craton in Western Australia. This dataset will be released openly. Second, we deploy a generative adversarial network architecture — which we call BioGAN — that simultaneously learns to generate synthetic biosignature spectra and discriminate them from abiotic controls, achieving classification accuracy of 94.3 percent in preliminary trials on held-out Atacama samples. Third, we integrate uncertainty quantification. Our Bayesian wrapper provides confidence intervals for every prediction, ensuring that mission scientists can assess reliability before committing rover resources to drilling operations.

## Open Science Commitment

All code lives on GitHub. Our datasets, trained model weights, preprocessing pipelines, and evaluation benchmarks will be published under CC-BY-4.0 and Apache 2.0 licenses within six months of each project milestone, ensuring that the broader astrobiology and machine learning communities can reproduce, critique, and extend every aspect of our work. Transparency drives better science.

## Timeline and Milestones

Phase one spans months one through eight. During this phase, we complete analog site sampling in Western Australia and finalize the hyperspectral database architecture with full metadata documentation. Phase two is model development. Months nine through eighteen focus on BioGAN training, hyperparameter optimization, and adversarial robustness testing against twelve known spectral interference categories. Phase three delivers mission readiness. We validate against archived MRO CRISM orbital data and produce a deployment-ready software package suitable for integration with ExoMars and Mars Sample Return instrument pipelines.

## Budget Overview

We request €485,000 over twenty-four months. Field campaigns require €127,000. Computational infrastructure — including GPU cluster access at SURFsara and cloud overflow — requires €93,000, while personnel costs for two postdoctoral researchers and one data engineer total €218,000 across the project duration. The remaining €47,000 covers open-access publication fees, conference dissemination, and community workshop hosting.

## Why This Matters Now

Mars exploration enters a pivotal decade. The convergence of next-generation spectrometers aboard planned missions, exponentially growing computational capacity, and maturing adversarial machine learning techniques creates an unprecedented opportunity to automate biosignature detection at scales and sensitivities impossible just five years ago. We must build these tools openly. Closed, proprietary approaches to astrobiology's most profound question — whether life exists beyond Earth — would represent a failure of scientific values at precisely the moment when collaborative, transparent discovery becomes technically feasible.

**Contact:** c.veltheim@uu.nl | ORCID: 0000-0003-7712-4458

# Grant Application: Deep Science Ventures — Planetary Science Division

## Project Title: Spectral Fingerprinting of Subsurface Biosignatures on Enceladus Using Federated Machine Learning

## Principal Investigator: Dr. Raina Vasquez-Okonkwo, University of Cascadia Institute for Planetary Systems

---

## Summary

Enceladus harbors a hidden ocean. Our project will deploy federated machine learning architectures to identify subtle spectral biosignatures in existing Cassini INMS data that traditional analysis pipelines have systematically overlooked for nearly two decades. Life may leave faint traces. We propose that amino acid precursors and phospholipid degradation products create identifiable spectral interference patterns when analyzed through our novel ensemble decomposition framework, which we call Distributed Astrobiological Signal Extraction, or DASE.

## Problem Statement

Current biosignature detection relies on rigid thresholds. Researchers typically search for specific molecular masses in mass spectrometry data, but this approach fundamentally ignores the complex combinatorial chemistry that biological systems produce in dilute, high-salinity hydrothermal environments beneath icy crusts. We are missing the forest. Our preliminary analysis of publicly available Cassini flyby E21 data revealed seventeen anomalous spectral clusters between 50 and 200 daltons that correlate strongly with theorized metabolic byproducts of chemolithoautotrophic organisms, yet no published study has investigated these clusters systematically.

## Technical Approach

DASE operates across three integrated layers. First, our preprocessing module applies adaptive wavelet denoising calibrated against laboratory spectra from our simulated Enceladean hydrothermal vent chamber, which operates at 2.1 GPa and 387K at the Cascadia Geochemistry Annex. Signal clarity improves dramatically. Second, our federated learning architecture enables fourteen partner institutions across nine countries to collaboratively train classification models without sharing raw proprietary datasets, preserving data sovereignty while maximizing statistical power across heterogeneous spectral libraries. The third layer synthesizes everything. An interpretable attention-based neural network generates ranked biosignature candidate lists with full uncertainty quantification, confidence intervals, and provenance tracking that satisfies emerging open science reproducibility standards.

## Open Science Commitment

Transparency drives our methodology completely. All trained models, synthetic training datasets, preprocessing code, and analysis notebooks will be released under CC-BY-4.0 and Apache 2.0 licenses through our dedicated Zenodo community repository within sixty days of each project milestone completion. We reject closed-door planetary science. Our federated architecture was specifically designed so that institutions in the Global South can participate fully without expensive data transfer infrastructure, requiring only modest computational resources equivalent to a standard university workstation.

## Budget Overview

We request £485,000 over thirty months. Personnel costs constitute £290,000, covering two postdoctoral researchers, one machine learning engineer, and one part-time data steward responsible for maintaining our open repositories and community engagement. Hardware needs are modest. Our federated training protocol requires a central coordination server plus cloud computing credits estimated at £65,000, while the remaining £130,000 covers laboratory validation experiments, partner coordination workshops, and conference dissemination activities.

## Expected Impact

This project could reshape biosignature detection permanently. If DASE validates even three of our seventeen preliminary anomalous spectral clusters as consistent with biological origin chemistry, it would represent the strongest indirect evidence for extraterrestrial life ever produced from existing mission data. The implications extend far beyond Enceladus. Our federated framework establishes reusable infrastructure for collaborative analysis of Europa Clipper and Dragonfly mission data, positioning Deep Science Ventures at the vanguard of a methodological revolution in planetary science that prioritizes openness, equity, and computational rigor over institutional gatekeeping.

We stand at an extraordinary threshold. The evidence for life beyond Earth may already exist in our archives, waiting for the right analytical lens.

---

*Application submitted by Dr. R. Vasquez-Okonkwo | Variant 5 | Contact: r.vasquez-okonkwo@ucips.ac.uk*

# Grant Application: Deep Science Ventures — Open Call

## Mapping Subsurface Brine Networks on Mars Using Ensemble Spectral Inversion

**PI: Dr. Casey Aldrin-Novak | Affiliation: Independent Researcher (prev. Caltech GPS Division)**

---

### What I'm Actually Trying to Do

I'm building a pipeline that takes MARSIS and SHARAD radar sounder data — the stuff we've been collecting from Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter and Mars Express for almost two decades now — and applies ensemble ML inversion to map probable subsurface brine reservoirs at depths between 1.2 and 4.8 km beneath the Martian south polar layered deposits.

The specific trigger for this was Lauro et al. (2021) walking back some of the earlier claims about liquid water under the south pole. Their argument was that the bright basal reflections could be explained by clays or metallic minerals. Fair point. But nobody's systematically tested whether a trained model can actually distinguish between brine interfaces and mineral dielectric anomalies using the full spectral envelope rather than just peak reflection amplitude. That's the gap I'm working in — not groundbreaking on its face, but I think it genuinely matters for astrobiology target selection.

### What I've Done So Far

I built a synthetic radargram generator that simulates MARSIS returns for layered permafrost with embedded brine lenses of varying salinity (calcium perchlorate concentrations from 7% to saturation at ~52% by weight). I trained a gradient-boosted ensemble on about 340,000 synthetic traces, then validated against 12,000 real MARSIS orbit tracks from the Ultimi Scopuli region. Early results are promising — the model flags 23 candidate brine zones that don't overlap with known clay deposits mapped by CRISM, and it rejects about 60% of the previously reported "bright spots" as likely mineralogical artifacts.

The hard part was getting the dielectric model right. I spent four months on a three-layer lossy medium simulation before I realized my attenuation estimates for CO₂ clathrate were off by a factor of ~1.8, which was contaminating everything downstream. Humbling. I've since recalibrated against lab permittivity measurements from Stillman & Grimm (2011) and the results stabilized.

### What the Funding Would Cover

I'm requesting **£85,000 over 18 months** to:

- Purchase dedicated GPU compute time (~£22,000) to extend the model to SHARAD's higher-frequency returns and fuse both datasets
- Hire one part-time research software engineer (£48,000) to refactor the pipeline into a publicly accessible tool — I want any planetary scientist to be able to upload an orbit track and get a brine probability map back
- Cover travel to two conferences and one lab visit to Tor Vergata (Rome), where the MARSIS team is based, to ground-truth my synthetic calibration (£9,000)
- Open-access publication fees and archiving the full model/training data on Zenodo (~£6,000)

### Why This Fits DSV's Mission

This is an astrobiology-adjacent infrastructure project. If we can't reliably distinguish water from rock at depth on Mars, we're flying partially blind when selecting drill sites for future missions — Icebreaker, Mars Life Explorer, whatever actually gets funded. I didn't set out to build a mission-planning tool, but that's kind of what it's becoming. Every piece of it — code, training data, model weights — will be open from day one.

I don't think I'm solving the "is there life on Mars" question. I'm trying to make the next person's attempt at answering it slightly less noisy.

---

**Contact:** c.aldrin-novak@protonmail.com | ORCID: 0000-0002-XXXX-XXXX

# I Accidentally Learned About Tardigrades and Now I Can't Shut Up About Them

So I fell down a Wikipedia rabbit hole last Tuesday — you know, the kind where you start by looking up whether you can microwave aluminum foil (you can't, past me was an idiot) and somehow end up reading about microscopic bears that can survive the vacuum of space. Yeah. Let me tell about tardigrades.

These little guys are everywhere. I mean *everywhere*. Your backyard, the ocean floor, probably your roof gutters right now. They're about half a millimeter long, they've got eight pudgy legs, and they look like someone designed a gummy bear while extremely sleep-deprived. I'm honestly kind of jealous of how unbothered they look.

But here's where it gets wild. Tardigrades can survive conditions that would absolutely obliterate me (and I mean, a strong breeze can ruin my whole day, so the bar isn't high). We're talking temperatures close to absolute zero. We're talking 300 degrees Fahrenheit. Pressure six times greater than the deepest ocean trenches. *Radiation* at doses hundreds of times what would kill a human. Scientists even shot them into actual outer space in 2007, and a bunch of them survived. I can't even survive a red-eye flight without complaining for three days.

Their secret is this thing called cryptobiosis. Basically, when conditions get terrible, tardigrades curl up into a little ball called a "tun," shut down their metabolism to nearly zero percent, and just... wait. They replace the water in their cells with a sugar called trehalose, which forms a glass-like structure that protects everything inside. They can stay like this for *decades* and then just rehydrate and waddle off like nothing happened.

I've been thinking about this nonstop. There's an organism on this planet that laughed off the five major mass extinctions — including the one that killed the dinosaurs — and it's smaller than a period at the end of a sentence. Meanwhile, I got a sunburn last weekend because I forgot to reapply sunscreen.

Scientists are actually studying tardigrade proteins now to figure out how they pull this off, hoping it could help with things like preserving vaccines without refrigeration.

Nature is absolutely unhinged and I'm here for it.

# I Accidentally Learned About Octopus Brains and Now I Can't Stop Thinking About Them

So I fell down a YouTube rabbit hole last night — you know, the kind where you start watching a video about meal prep and somehow end up at 2 AM learning about cephalopod neuroscience. Classic me.

Here's the thing that broke my brain (pun absolutely intended): octopuses don't have one brain. They have NINE. Well, kind of. They've got a central brain between their eyes, and then each of their eight arms has its own mini-brain, basically a large cluster of neurons that can act independently. That means an arm can taste, touch, and decide to grab something without the central brain even knowing about it.

I'm over here struggling to pat my head and rub my stomach at the same time, and these guys are simultaneously opening jars, solving puzzles, and probably filing their taxes with all eight arms.

What really gets me is the implications for how we think about consciousness and intelligence. Like, is each arm... aware? That's a weird question, I know. But two-thirds of an octopus's neurons are in its arms, not its central brain. So when an arm explores a crevice looking for food, is that the octopus deciding to do that, or is the arm just doing its own thing? Where does "the octopus" even begin and end?

And here's what nobody talks about enough — their neurons aren't even organized the same way ours are. Mammal brains are all about centralized processing. Everything gets routed up to headquarters. But octopuses evolved intelligence completely separately from us. It's like nature ran the experiment twice and got two wildly different answers to the same question.

I'll be honest, I'm not a neurobiologist. I'm just a person who should've gone to bed at a reasonable hour. But I think that's what makes this stuff so fascinating to me. We spend so much time assuming intelligence looks like *our* intelligence — centralized, skull-based, verbal. Meanwhile, there's a boneless sea creature out there with nine brains unscrewing childproof containers.

Makes you humble, honestly. Or at least it should.

text:
# Grant Application: Automated Detection of Subsurface Brine Migration Patterns on Mars Using Ensemble Neural Networks

**Applicant:** Dr. Kira Vasquez-Okoye, Planetary Geochemistry Lab, University of New South Wales

---

## The Problem

We've known since the MARSIS and SHARAD orbital radar campaigns that Mars has a complicated subsurface story, but honestly, the way we currently process radargram data to look for transient liquid brine signatures is embarrassingly manual. A trained researcher stares at thousands of radar cross-sections, flags "interesting" reflectivity anomalies, and then argues about them at conferences for years. That's basically how the debate over the Ultimi Scopuli bright reflections has played out since 2018 — smart people squinting at the same data and reaching opposite conclusions.

I want to fix the squinting part.

## What I'm Building

I've been developing an ensemble ML pipeline — I'm calling it **BrineNet** (not groundbreaking, but it works) — that ingests MARSIS subsurface radargrams and classifies basal reflectivity anomalies by their likelihood of representing liquid brines versus dry geological explanations like smectite clay deposits or high-density iron oxides.

The architecture combines three models: a 1D convolutional network that processes individual radar traces for amplitude and phase behavior, a recurrent module (bidirectional LSTM) that captures spatial continuity across adjacent traces, and a gradient-boosted decision tree that incorporates contextual features like surface temperature estimates from TES/THEMIS, local crustal heat flow models, and perchlorate concentration predictions from GRS data.

I trained the initial version on ~14,000 synthetic radargrams I generated using a dielectric mixing model for Martian regolith compositions. The tricky part was making the synthetic training data dirty enough to match real MARSIS noise characteristics — I spent three months just getting the ionospheric distortion simulation right, and I'm still not totally happy with it.

Early results are promising. When I ran BrineNet against the South Polar Layered Deposits dataset, it flagged 23 high-confidence brine candidate regions. Seventeen overlap with previously published detections. Six are new. Three of those six sit along a thermal gradient that's consistent with localized geothermal activity, which is... genuinely exciting and also terrifying because I keep second-guessing whether I've introduced a bias.

## What I Need

I'm requesting **£85,000 over 18 months** to:

- Expand the synthetic training set to 200,000+ radargrams covering a wider range of subsurface compositions (£12,000 compute costs)
- Hire a postdoc with radar signal processing expertise to validate the ionospheric correction module (£58,000 salary + benefits)
- Run a blind validation study where the model's predictions are compared against independent analyses from three external planetary radar groups (£8,000 coordination costs)
- Publish all code, trained models, and training data on an open repository with full documentation (£7,000 for data hosting and curation)

## Why This Matters for Astrobiology

If liquid brines exist in the Martian subsurface today, that's the single most important habitability constraint we can pin down before sending life-detection missions. But we can't design those missions well if we're still arguing about whether the brines are real. BrineNet won't settle the argument by itself — I'd be delusional to claim that — but it can move the conversation from "does this one reflector look bright to you?" to "here's a systematic, reproducible, probabilistic map of every candidate brine deposit on Mars."

Everything gets published openly. Every weight, every training sample, every line of preprocessing code. If I've made a mistake, I want people to find it fast.

So I fell down a YouTube rabbit hole last night — you know, the kind where you start watching a video about meal prep and somehow end up at 2 AM learning about cephalopod neuroscience. Classic me.

Here's the thing that broke my brain (pun absolutely intended): octopuses don't have one brain. They have NINE. Well, kind of. They've got a central brain between their eyes, and then each of their eight arms has its own mini-brain, basically a large cluster of neurons that can act independently. That means an arm can taste, touch, and decide to grab something without the central brain even knowing about it.

I'm over here struggling to pat my head and rub my stomach at the same time, and these guys are simultaneously opening jars, solving puzzles, and probably filing their taxes with all eight arms.

What really gets me is the implications for how we think about consciousness and intelligence. Like, is each arm... aware? That's a weird question, I know. But two-thirds of an octopus's neurons are in its arms, not its central brain. So when an arm explores a crevice looking for food, is that the octopus deciding to do that, or is the arm just doing its own thing? Where does "the octopus" even begin and end?

And here's what nobody talks about enough — their neurons aren't even organized the same way ours are. Mammal brains are all about centralized processing. Everything gets routed up to headquarters. But octopuses evolved intelligence completely separately from us. It's like nature ran the experiment twice and got two wildly different answers to the same question.

I'll be honest, I'm not a neurobiologist. I'm just a person who should've gone to bed at a reasonable hour. But I think that's what makes this stuff so fascinating to me. We spend so much time assuming intelligence looks like *our* intelligence — centralized, skull-based, verbal. Meanwhile, there's a boneless sea creature out there with nine brains unscrewing childproof containers.

Makes you humble, honestly. Or at least it should.

# Grant Application: Automated Classification of Subsurface Mineralogical Signatures in Martian Regolith Using Sparse Spectral Data

**Applicant:** Dr. Nadia Okoye-Ferris | Planetary Geochemistry & Machine Learning Lab, Utrecht

---

## The Problem

We have a spectral data bottleneck, and honestly, it's kind of embarrassing. Mars orbiters and rovers have been collecting VNIR and thermal emission spectra of regolith for over two decades now, and we're still relying on semi-manual classification pipelines that were essentially designed for Earth-based remote sensing. I've spent the last three years watching colleagues in my department hand-label phyllosilicate vs. sulfate mineral assemblages from CRISM hyperspectral strips — thousands of hours of expert time that doesn't scale and isn't reproducible.

The real issue isn't that we lack data. It's that Martian subsurface mineralogy, the stuff that actually matters for understanding past water activity and potential habitability, shows up as faint, noisy spectral features buried under dust coating and atmospheric CO₂ absorption bands. Standard dimensionality reduction chokes on this. I've tried PCA on CRISM targeted observations from Mawrth Vallis and the signal-to-noise ratio for smectite subclasses basically collapses below 2.3 μm.

## What I Want to Build

I'm proposing a sparse Bayesian spectral unmixing model — not groundbreaking in the machine learning sense, but applied in a way nobody's actually done for this specific problem. The idea is to train on synthetic mineral mixtures generated from the RELAB and PDS spectral libraries (about 14,000 lab-measured endmember spectra), then fine-tune on the ~800 expert-labeled CRISM scenes where we have reasonable ground truth from Curiosity and Perseverance traverse data.

The model uses a variational autoencoder backbone with a sparsity prior on the latent mineral abundance vectors. I built a rough prototype last summer that handled binary classification (hydrated vs. anhydrous) with 91% accuracy on held-out synthetic data. That's the easy version, though. The hard part is going from binary to multi-class subgroup identification — distinguishing Fe/Mg-smectites from Al-smectites from chlorites — because their spectral features overlap in the 1.4–1.9 μm range and real orbital data has maybe 18 nm spectral resolution at best.

## Why This Matters for Astrobiology Target Selection

Future sample-return and life-detection missions need to pick landing sites based on subsurface mineral assemblages that indicate sustained liquid water. Right now, those decisions involve months of committee debate over ambiguous spectral maps. If I can get this classifier working reliably on sparse, noisy orbital data, it would let mission planners generate probabilistic habitability maps for candidate sites in days instead of months.

I'm also committing to releasing the full training pipeline, model weights, and labeled dataset under CC-BY-4.0. I've already set up a public repository for the synthetic spectral library generator. Open science isn't a checkbox for me — it's how I got into this work in the first place, using other people's openly shared CRISM processing scripts when I was a postdoc with zero budget.

## Budget & Timeline

I'm requesting €185,000 over 18 months. Roughly €72,000 covers a dedicated postdoc (spectral geochemistry background), €48,000 for GPU compute on the Dutch national cluster, €35,000 for my partial salary buyout, and the remainder for two workshops to get planetary scientists actually stress-testing the tool on real mapping problems.

Honestly, the model might not work perfectly for all mineral subclasses. I expect chlorite identification to remain shaky. But even a partial success — reliable automated smectite subgroup mapping across the Martian highlands — would save the community thousands of labor hours and make landing site selection significantly less subjective.

I'd rather ship something useful than promise something perfect.

# I Accidentally Learned About Tardigrades and Now I Can't Shut Up About Them

So I fell down a Wikipedia rabbit hole last Tuesday — you know, the kind where you start by looking up whether you can microwave aluminum foil (you can't, past me was an idiot) and somehow end up reading about microscopic bears that can survive the vacuum of space. Yeah. Let me tell about tardigrades.

These little guys are everywhere. I mean *everywhere*. Your backyard, the ocean floor, probably your roof gutters right now. They're about half a millimeter long, they've got eight pudgy legs, and they look like someone designed a gummy bear while extremely sleep-deprived. I'm honestly kind of jealous of how unbothered they look.

But here's where it gets wild. Tardigrades can survive conditions that would absolutely obliterate me (and I mean, a strong breeze can ruin my whole day, so the bar isn't high). We're talking temperatures close to absolute zero. We're talking 300 degrees Fahrenheit. Pressure six times greater than the deepest ocean trenches. *Radiation* at doses hundreds of times what would kill a human. Scientists even shot them into actual outer space in 2007, and a bunch of them survived. I can't even survive a red-eye flight without complaining for three days.

Their secret is this thing called cryptobiosis. Basically, when conditions get terrible, tardigrades curl up into a little ball called a "tun," shut down their metabolism to nearly zero percent, and just... wait. They replace the water in their cells with a sugar called trehalose, which forms a glass-like structure that protects everything inside. They can stay like this for *decades* and then just rehydrate and waddle off like nothing happened.

I've been thinking about this nonstop. There's an organism on this planet that laughed off the five major mass extinctions — including the one that killed the dinosaurs — and it's smaller than a period at the end of a sentence. Meanwhile, I got a sunburn last weekend because I forgot to reapply sunscreen.

Scientists are actually studying tardigrade proteins now to figure out how they pull this off, hoping it could help with things like preserving vaccines without refrigeration.

Nature is absolutely unhinged and I'm here for it.

# Grant Application: Deep Science Ventures — Open Call

## Automated Detection of Biosignature False Positives in Exoplanet Transit Spectra

**PI: Dr. Jordan Achebe | Submitted: June 2025**

---

### The Problem Nobody Wants to Talk About

I've spent the last three years watching our community get excited about atmospheric biosignatures — methane on K2-18b, phosphine on Venus (briefly), dimethyl sulfide hints from JWST — and then watching the quiet retractions and caveats roll in weeks later. The issue isn't that our instruments are bad. The issue is that we don't have a systematic way to flag when an abiotic process can reproduce the same spectral feature, and we keep getting burned by it.

So I built a classifier that tries to solve this, or at least make the problem less embarrassing.

### What I Actually Did

The core of this work is a neural network ensemble — not groundbreaking, but it works — trained on synthetic transmission spectra generated from two separate forward models: one assuming biological surface/atmospheric sources, and one assuming purely geochemical and photochemical origins. I used VULCAN for the photochemistry and a modified version of petitRADTRANS for the radiative transfer, generating about 1.4 million spectra across a parameter space covering M-dwarf and K-dwarf hosted rocky planets from 0.7 to 1.8 Earth radii.

The hard part was building the abiotic training set. Everyone wants to model biology; nobody wants to exhaustively catalog all the boring ways serpentinization, volcanic outgassing, and UV-driven photolysis can mimic a biosignature. I ended up parameterizing 23 distinct abiotic production pathways for CH₄, N₂O, and CH₃Cl, pulling reaction rate data from the NIST kinetics database and about forty papers' worth of experimental geochemistry results that I had to digitize manually because half of them didn't have machine-readable supplementary data. That was a fun month.

The classifier outputs a false-positive probability score for any input spectrum at JWST/NIRSpec resolution, and I've validated it against a held-out test set with an AUC of 0.91. It catches things. Specifically, it's good at identifying when a methane-CO₂ co-detection is more consistent with iron-rich hydrothermal systems than with methanogenesis, which is exactly the scenario that'll come up repeatedly as we push into the TRAPPIST-1 characterization era.

### What I'm Asking For

I'm requesting £85,000 over 18 months to:

- **Expand the abiotic pathway library** from 23 to ~60 mechanisms, incorporating recent experimental work on sulfur-cycling false positives and prebiotic organics delivery via impacts
- **Integrate real JWST Early Release and Cycle 2 data** to test against actual instrument systematics rather than idealized noise models
- **Release the full pipeline as open-source software** with a public API, because I genuinely think this should be a standard preprocessing step before anyone publishes a biosignature claim

I didn't build this to replace human judgment. I built it so the atmospheric retrieval community has one more sanity check before the press releases go out.

### Why This Fits DSV's Model

This sits at the intersection of planetary science, astrobiology, and ML, and I'm committed to open science — the training data, model weights, and validation notebooks will all go on Zenodo with a CC-BY license. The tool is instrument-agnostic enough that it'll extend to ELT and HWO-era spectroscopy without a full rebuild.

I think the next decade of exoplanet science will be defined less by discovery and more by interpretation. This is a small contribution to making that interpretation honest.

text:
# Grant Application: Automated Detection of Biosignature False Positives in Exoplanet Transmission Spectra

**PI: Dr. Kira Vasquez-Okafor | Submitted to Deep Science Ventures, Open Call 2024**

---

## The Problem Nobody Wants to Talk About

We're getting really good at detecting atmospheric signatures on exoplanets. Maybe too good. Every few months, someone publishes a paper claiming a tentative phosphine or methane detection on a rocky world, the press runs with it, and then six months later a quieter paper explains it was probably stellar contamination or an instrument artifact. I've watched this cycle repeat at least four times since 2021, and it's starting to erode public and institutional trust in astrobiology claims.

I built a small prototype last year that flags likely false positives in transmission spectra by cross-referencing the detection against a database of known instrumental systematics and stellar activity patterns. Not groundbreaking, but it works — it correctly identified 87% of historically retracted or revised biosignature claims when I fed it archival JWST and Hubble data from 23 transiting exoplanets. The hard part was training it to distinguish between "this molecule genuinely shouldn't be here" and "this looks weird because the star was flaring during transit observation three."

## What I'm Proposing

I want to scale this into **SPECTRE** (Spectral Plausibility Engine for Chemical and Transmission Reliability Evaluation) — an open-source ML pipeline that sits between raw spectral reduction and published biosignature claims. Think of it as a skepticism layer.

Specifically, I'm planning to:

1. **Expand the training corpus** to include synthetic spectra generated from 1D atmospheric models (I've been using CHIMERA and petitRADTRANS) injected with realistic noise profiles from JWST NIRSpec and MIRI, plus ground-based high-resolution instruments like ESPRESSO.

2. **Build a contamination library** cataloging ~400 known sources of spectral false positives — everything from unocculted starspots to overlapping molecular absorption bands that mimic biosignatures. I started this manually and I'm at about 160 entries. It's tedious work, honestly.

3. **Release the whole thing as an open tool** with a simple API so that any team preparing a biosignature paper can run their detection through SPECTRE before submission. I don't want this locked behind institutional access.

## Why This Matters Right Now

JWST is producing an unprecedented volume of transmission spectroscopy data. The Ariel mission launches in 2029 and will observe ~1,000 exoplanet atmospheres. We don't currently have a standardized, automated way to stress-test biosignature claims before they enter the literature. Peer review catches some of this, but reviewers are overworked and often aren't specialists in instrument systematics.

I tried pitching this as a side project within my department and got the usual response — interesting idea, no funding line for it. DSV's focus on planetary science and open tooling is honestly the reason I'm writing this application instead of shelving it.

## Budget and Timeline

I'm requesting **£85,000 over 18 months**. Roughly £42,000 covers a postdoctoral researcher (12 months, 0.8 FTE) with experience in atmospheric retrieval. £18,000 goes to cloud compute for training and hosting the API. £15,000 for two collaborative workshops bringing in spectroscopists and ML people to validate the contamination library. The remaining £10,000 covers publication costs and conference dissemination.

## What Success Looks Like

By month 18, SPECTRE should be publicly available on GitHub with documentation, a peer-reviewed methods paper submitted, and at least three external research groups beta-testing it against their own unpublished detections. I didn't set out to build something that kills exciting discoveries — I want to make sure the ones that survive scrutiny actually deserve the excitement.

text:
# Grant Application: Spectral Cartography of Subsurface Brines via Adversarial Generative Priors

## Project Title
**HALOSYNTH: Machine-Learned Halophilic Biosignature Detection in Cryogenic Evaporite Terrains**

## Principal Investigator
Dr. Maren Voss-Okoro, Exogeochemistry Division, Nordic Astrobiology Consortium

---

## Summary

Chloride-bearing evaporite deposits on Mars and Europa's linea formations represent compelling astrobiological targets, yet current spectral unmixing pipelines struggle to disambiguate abiotic mineral assemblages from subtle organic-mineral co-precipitates. HALOSYNTH proposes a paradigm departure: leveraging conditional generative adversarial networks (cGANs) trained on laboratory-synthesized cryogenic brine spectra to construct probabilistic biosignature maps across planetary hyperspectral datasets.

## Scientific Rationale

Terrestrial halophilic archaea—particularly *Halobacterium salinarum* and *Salinibacter ruber*—produce bacterioruberin pigments exhibiting diagnostic absorption features near 470–550 nm. When entombed within polyhydrated sulfate or hydrohalite matrices under cryogenic conditions (<230 K), these chromophores undergo bathochromic shifts that conventional linear spectral deconvolution algorithms routinely misclassify as ferric oxide contamination. No existing autonomous detection framework accounts for temperature-dependent spectral metamorphism of biogenic carotenoids embedded in crystalline host phases.

## Methodology

**Phase I — Cryogenic Analogue Synthesis:** We will precipitate ternary NaCl–MgSO₄–perchlorate eutectics doped with bacterioruberin concentrates inside an environmental vacuum chamber cycling between 150–273 K. Reflectance measurements via fiber-coupled VNIR-SWIR spectrometers will generate approximately 18,000 labeled endmember signatures spanning biotic-abiotic compositional gradients.

**Phase II — Adversarial Spectral Augmentation:** A Wasserstein cGAN architecture will synthesize physically plausible interpolated spectra, expanding training manifolds beyond laboratory sampling limitations. Crucially, the discriminator incorporates radiative transfer constraints derived from Hapke bidirectional reflectance theory, preventing hallucinated outputs violating photometric reciprocity.

**Phase III — Orbital Deployment:** Trained models will ingest CRISM (MRO) targeted observations of Meridiani Planum chloride deposits and, prospectively, Europa Clipper MISE datacubes. Bayesian posterior probability surfaces quantifying biosignature likelihood at each pixel will be disseminated through an open-access PostGIS geodatabase with full provenance metadata.

## Open Science Commitment

All synthetic spectral libraries, network architectures, and pretrained weights will be deposited on Zenodo under CC-BY-4.0 licensing within 90 days of each milestone. Reproducibility capsules via Code Ocean will accompany every publication. We additionally commit to preregistering analytical decisions on the Open Science Framework before commencing orbital classification.

## Budget Justification (£385,000 over 24 months)

| Category | Allocation |
|---|---|
| Cryogenic chamber instrumentation & consumables | £95,000 |
| Postdoctoral researcher (computational geosciences) | £112,000 |
| GPU cluster allocation (NVIDIA A100 nodes) | £58,000 |
| Fieldwork, Atacama salar ground-truth campaign | £47,000 |
| Open-data infrastructure & archival | £28,000 |
| Conference dissemination & stakeholder workshops | £24,000 |
| Institutional overhead (13%) | £21,000 |

## Expected Impact

HALOSYNTH establishes transferable methodological infrastructure applicable wherever cryogenic mineralogy intersects habitability assessment. Beyond immediate Martian relevance, the adversarial prior framework offers a scalable template for Enceladean plume spectroscopy and Titan tholin discrimination. By releasing validated synthetic training corpora, we lower entry barriers for under-resourced groups pursuing astrobiology machine-learning research—directly catalyzing the collaborative ecosystem Deep Science Ventures champions.

# Unraveling the Silicate Mantles: Fresh Perspectives on Terrestrial Differentiation

Planetary science captivates researchers who dedicate their careers to deciphering how celestial bodies originate, evolve, and ultimately transform across cosmological timescales. Geochemical fingerprints preserved within chondritic meteorites reveal primordial compositions predating our solar nebula's gravitational collapse. These extraterrestrial specimens harbor presolar grains—crystalline relics forged inside ancient stellar envelopes billions of years before Earth's accretion.

Magma ocean hypotheses suggest that protoplanetary embryos underwent wholesale melting during oligarchic growth phases, facilitating siderophile element sequestration into metallic cores. Rheological properties governing silicate melt viscosity profoundly influenced crystallization sequences throughout such global-scale fractionation episodes. Incompatible lithophile species consequently enriched residual liquids, generating compositionally stratified reservoirs detectable via modern isotopic systematics.

Venus presents particularly enigmatic puzzles because its resurfacing chronology remains contentious among volcanologists studying tessera highlands and coronae structures. Magellan radar altimetry mapped extensive basaltic plains exhibiting remarkably uniform crater distributions, implying catastrophic lithospheric overturn approximately five hundred million years ago. Stagnant-lid convection models challenge mobilist tectonic paradigms traditionally applied when interpreting venusian geological provinces.

Beyond rocky worlds, cryovolcanic phenomena on Saturnian satellites demonstrate that volatile-rich interiors can sustain geological activity without radiogenic heating alone. Enceladus's spectacular south-polar plumes eject saline hydrothermal fluids through fractured ice shells, hinting at subsurface habitable environments maintained by tidal dissipation. Cassini's mass spectrometry detected complex macromolecular organic compounds within those gaseous jets—an astounding discovery energizing astrobiological inquiry.

Martian paleoclimate reconstructions increasingly invoke episodic atmospheric thickening driven by obliquity oscillations and massive volcanic degassing from Tharsis province eruptions. Lacustrine sedimentary deposits identified by orbital hyperspectral instruments confirm sustained liquid water stability during Noachian-Hesperian transitional epochs. Phyllosilicate mineral assemblages discovered at Jezero crater's deltaic exposures motivated Perseverance rover's ambitious sample-caching mission architecture.

Exoplanetary characterization now extends these foundational principles toward distant super-Earths orbiting M-dwarf stellar hosts. Transit spectrophotometry enables atmospheric retrieval algorithms to constrain molecular abundances, cloud deck pressures, and thermal gradient profiles simultaneously. Such revolutionary observational capabilities promise unprecedented comparative planetological insights throughout coming decades, fundamentally reshaping humanity's understanding of world-building processes operating galaxywide.

# Whispers from the Outer Reaches

Beneath Jupiter's turbulent ammonia cirrus, pressures escalate until hydrogen undergoes a remarkable metamorphosis into metallic liquid—an exotic phase generating the strongest magnetosphere among our celestial neighborhood's wanderers. This dynamo mechanism remains partially enigmatic, though Juno's perijove measurements have illuminated previously obscure interior dynamics.

Cryovolcanism on Enceladus presents another captivating puzzle. Tremendous tidal flexing, orchestrated by Saturnian gravitational resonances, fractures the moon's siliceous shell along characteristic "tiger stripe" sulci. Subsurface briny reservoirs subsequently erupt through these fissures, ejecting particulate geysers containing complex organochemical signatures that tantalize astrobiologists worldwide.

Consider also Venus's superrotating atmosphere, where zonal winds circumnavigate the globe approximately sixty times faster than the sluggish lithospheric rotation below. Sulfuric acid aerosols enshroud everything, creating a catastrophic greenhouse regime maintaining surface temperatures sufficient to liquefy lead. Recent phosphine detection claims—albeit controversially disputed—reignited speculation about possible aerial microbial habitats suspended within temperate cloud decks.

Mars tells a fundamentally different geological narrative. Ancient valley networks, phyllosilicate mineral deposits, and deltaic sedimentary formations collectively testify to prolonged Noachian-era hydrological activity. Somewhere during the Hesperian transition, atmospheric stripping by solar wind—unimpeded without a protective geomagnetic shield—gradually desiccated the Martian environment into today's hyperarid wasteland.

Farther afield, Kuiper Belt objects like Arrokoth reveal pristine accretionary architecture unchanged since nebular condensation epochs. Their bilobed contact-binary morphology suggests extraordinarily gentle coalescence velocities, challenging earlier collisional aggregation models that predicted violent fragmentation during planetesimal assembly.

Exoplanetary discoveries continually reshape our taxonomic frameworks. Hot Neptunes occupying the Savanna desert, sub-Saturnian worlds exhibiting radius valley characteristics, and tidally locked lava planets with magmatic hemispheric oceans—none were anticipated before radial velocity surveys commenced decades ago.

Isotopic fractionation patterns measured within chondritic meteorites meanwhile constrain protoplanetary disk thermochemistry, connecting laboratory geochemistry with astronomical observations spanning parsecs. Refractory calcium-aluminum inclusions represent arguably the oldest datable solids, crystallized approximately 4.567 billion years prior.

# Grant Application: Deep Science Ventures — Planetary Science & Astrobiology

## Project Title: **Chemolithotrophic Signature Detection via Adversarial Spectral Networks on Enceladean Plume Analogs**

### Principal Investigator: Dr. Maren Solvik, Astrobiochemistry Group, Nordvik Institute for Exoplanetary Studies

---

## 1. Executive Summary

Hydrothermal effluent from Enceladus's subsurface ocean represents arguably the most compelling astrobiological target within our solar system. Yet current mass spectrometry pipelines—designed primarily for Martian regolith geochemistry—lack sensitivity to discriminate abiotic serpentinization byproducts from genuine metabolic signatures of chemolithoautotrophic microorganisms. We propose constructing an open-source adversarial neural framework, **EnceladusNet**, trained on laboratory-synthesized plume analogs to disentangle biogenic from mineralogical spectral features at parts-per-trillion resolution.

## 2. Scientific Rationale

Cassini's Ion and Neutral Mass Spectrometer (INMS) detected molecular hydrogen, silica nanograins, and complex macromolecular organic compounds during Saturnian flyby transects—tantalizing circumstantial evidence for methanogenic or acetogenic metabolism near seafloor vents. However, degeneracy between Fischer-Tropsch-type synthesis and enzymatic catalysis produces nearly indistinguishable volatile fingerprints under conventional analytical paradigms.

Our preliminary computational experiments reveal that generative adversarial architectures, when furnished with sufficiently heterogeneous training corpora spanning alkaline hydrothermal chemistry, can isolate subtle stoichiometric anomalies—particularly non-equilibrium isotopologue ratios and enantiomeric excess in amino acid fragmentation patterns—that purely statistical methods overlook.

## 3. Methodology

**Phase I (Months 1–8):** Fabrication of analog brines mimicking Enceladean ocean parameters (pH ~11, dissolved NaCl/NaHCO₃, trace phosphorus) within titanium autoclaves at pressures replicating ~50 km ice-shell overburden. Biotic reactors will be inoculated with extremophilic *Methanothermococcus* strains; sterile controls undergo identical thermodynamic forcing. Ultrahigh-resolution Orbitrap spectrometry and Raman microprobe analysis generate paired labeled datasets.

**Phase II (Months 6–14):** Development of a conditional Wasserstein GAN architecture where the discriminator learns latent biosignature manifolds separating living-system outputs from abiogenic mineral assemblages. Transfer learning from terrestrial serpentinite-hosted ecosystems (Lost City hydrothermal field reference spectra) augments generalizability. Bayesian hyperparameter optimization ensures robustness against instrumental noise characteristic of spacecraft-grade detectors.

**Phase III (Months 12–18):** Community validation through an open benchmarking challenge hosted on Zenodo, releasing all synthetic spectral libraries, preprocessing notebooks, and trained model weights under CC-BY-4.0 licensing. Interoperability with NASA's Planetary Data System ensures downstream mission applicability.

## 4. Alignment with DSV Priorities

This venture bridges three convergent domains: **planetary habitability assessment**, **machine intelligence for scientific discovery**, and **radical openness** in data infrastructure. EnceladusNet directly informs detector requirements for proposed flagship missions (Enceladus Orbilander, Europa Clipper follow-on concepts) while establishing reproducible, community-governed ML benchmarks absent from current astrobiology toolkits.
