# Unraveling the Silicate Mantles: Fresh Perspectives on Terrestrial Differentiation

Planetary science captivates researchers who dedicate their careers to deciphering how celestial bodies originate, evolve, and ultimately transform across cosmological timescales. Geochemical fingerprints preserved within chondritic meteorites reveal primordial compositions predating our solar nebula's gravitational collapse. These extraterrestrial specimens harbor presolar grains—crystalline relics forged inside ancient stellar envelopes billions of years before Earth's accretion.

Magma ocean hypotheses suggest that protoplanetary embryos underwent wholesale melting during oligarchic growth phases, facilitating siderophile element sequestration into metallic cores. Rheological properties governing silicate melt viscosity profoundly influenced crystallization sequences throughout such global-scale fractionation episodes. Incompatible lithophile species consequently enriched residual liquids, generating compositionally stratified reservoirs detectable via modern isotopic systematics.

Venus presents particularly enigmatic puzzles because its resurfacing chronology remains contentious among volcanologists studying tessera highlands and coronae structures. Magellan radar altimetry mapped extensive basaltic plains exhibiting remarkably uniform crater distributions, implying catastrophic lithospheric overturn approximately five hundred million years ago. Stagnant-lid convection models challenge mobilist tectonic paradigms traditionally applied when interpreting venusian geological provinces.

Beyond rocky worlds, cryovolcanic phenomena on Saturnian satellites demonstrate that volatile-rich interiors can sustain geological activity without radiogenic heating alone. Enceladus's spectacular south-polar plumes eject saline hydrothermal fluids through fractured ice shells, hinting at subsurface habitable environments maintained by tidal dissipation. Cassini's mass spectrometry detected complex macromolecular organic compounds within those gaseous jets—an astounding discovery energizing astrobiological inquiry.

Martian paleoclimate reconstructions increasingly invoke episodic atmospheric thickening driven by obliquity oscillations and massive volcanic degassing from Tharsis province eruptions. Lacustrine sedimentary deposits identified by orbital hyperspectral instruments confirm sustained liquid water stability during Noachian-Hesperian transitional epochs. Phyllosilicate mineral assemblages discovered at Jezero crater's deltaic exposures motivated Perseverance rover's ambitious sample-caching mission architecture.

Exoplanetary characterization now extends these foundational principles toward distant super-Earths orbiting M-dwarf stellar hosts. Transit spectrophotometry enables atmospheric retrieval algorithms to constrain molecular abundances, cloud deck pressures, and thermal gradient profiles simultaneously. Such revolutionary observational capabilities promise unprecedented comparative planetological insights throughout coming decades, fundamentally reshaping humanity's understanding of world-building processes operating galaxywide.

# Whispers from the Outer Reaches

Beneath Jupiter's turbulent ammonia cirrus, pressures escalate until hydrogen undergoes a remarkable metamorphosis into metallic liquid—an exotic phase generating the strongest magnetosphere among our celestial neighborhood's wanderers. This dynamo mechanism remains partially enigmatic, though Juno's perijove measurements have illuminated previously obscure interior dynamics.

Cryovolcanism on Enceladus presents another captivating puzzle. Tremendous tidal flexing, orchestrated by Saturnian gravitational resonances, fractures the moon's siliceous shell along characteristic "tiger stripe" sulci. Subsurface briny reservoirs subsequently erupt through these fissures, ejecting particulate geysers containing complex organochemical signatures that tantalize astrobiologists worldwide.

Consider also Venus's superrotating atmosphere, where zonal winds circumnavigate the globe approximately sixty times faster than the sluggish lithospheric rotation below. Sulfuric acid aerosols enshroud everything, creating a catastrophic greenhouse regime maintaining surface temperatures sufficient to liquefy lead. Recent phosphine detection claims—albeit controversially disputed—reignited speculation about possible aerial microbial habitats suspended within temperate cloud decks.

Mars tells a fundamentally different geological narrative. Ancient valley networks, phyllosilicate mineral deposits, and deltaic sedimentary formations collectively testify to prolonged Noachian-era hydrological activity. Somewhere during the Hesperian transition, atmospheric stripping by solar wind—unimpeded without a protective geomagnetic shield—gradually desiccated the Martian environment into today's hyperarid wasteland.

Farther afield, Kuiper Belt objects like Arrokoth reveal pristine accretionary architecture unchanged since nebular condensation epochs. Their bilobed contact-binary morphology suggests extraordinarily gentle coalescence velocities, challenging earlier collisional aggregation models that predicted violent fragmentation during planetesimal assembly.

Exoplanetary discoveries continually reshape our taxonomic frameworks. Hot Neptunes occupying the Savanna desert, sub-Saturnian worlds exhibiting radius valley characteristics, and tidally locked lava planets with magmatic hemispheric oceans—none were anticipated before radial velocity surveys commenced decades ago.

Isotopic fractionation patterns measured within chondritic meteorites meanwhile constrain protoplanetary disk thermochemistry, connecting laboratory geochemistry with astronomical observations spanning parsecs. Refractory calcium-aluminum inclusions represent arguably the oldest datable solids, crystallized approximately 4.567 billion years prior.

Planetary science thus weaves mineralogy, fluid mechanics, orbital dynamics, and prebiotic chemistry into one magnificent interdisciplinary tapestry—perpetually expanding as robotic emissaries venture deeper into cosmic wilderness.

# Grant Application: Thermogenic Biosignature Detection via Adversarial Spectral Deconvolution

**Principal Investigator:** Dr. Inara Castellan, Computational Astrobiology Group, Meridian Institute for Exoplanetary Research

**Requested Funding:** £285,000 over 24 months

---

## Executive Summary

Identifying biogenic thermal anomalies within planetary regolith remains one of astrobiology's most confounding challenges. Current radiometric pipelines struggle to disambiguate metabolic heat signatures from purely geological exothermic processes—serpentinization, radioactive decay, and latent crystallization all produce overlapping infrared profiles. We propose **THERA** (Thermogenic Habitability Estimation through Robust Adversarial networks), a generative machine-learning framework designed to disentangle biological from abiotic thermal emissions across heterogeneous crustal substrates.

## Scientific Rationale

Subsurface chemolithotrophic organisms on Mars or Europa's silicate seafloor would inevitably dissipate metabolic waste heat. Although individually negligible, colonial aggregations of extremophilic microbiota could elevate localized temperatures by 0.02–0.15 K above ambient basaltic background—a differential theoretically resolvable by next-generation orbital spectrometers aboard missions such as ESA's EnVision and NASA's Europa Clipper.

However, no existing analytical toolkit adequately separates these faint thermogenic perturbations from confounding mineralogical noise. Conventional principal component analyses collapse under the curse of dimensionality when confronted with hyperspectral datacubes spanning 6–50 μm wavelength ranges at sub-kilometer spatial resolution.

## Technical Approach

THERA employs a novel conditional Wasserstein generative adversarial architecture with gradient-penalized discriminators trained on two complementary synthetic corpora:

1. **Abiotic Ensemble:** Forward-modeled emissivity spectra incorporating olivine weathering kinetics, phyllosilicate dehydroxylation, and sulfate paragenesis across variable albedo terrains, generated using our modified DISORT radiative transfer code.

2. **Biotic Perturbation Library:** Stochastic thermal injections calibrated against laboratory calorimetry of *Methanothermobacter thermautotrophicus*, *Deinococcus radiodurans*, and novel hyperthermophilic isolates cultured at analogous pressures.

The discriminator learns latent manifold boundaries separating these populations, while the generator continuously synthesizes increasingly ambiguous edge-case spectra—forcing adversarial co-evolution toward unprecedented classification granularity. Bayesian uncertainty quantification wraps each prediction, providing posterior credibility intervals essential for mission-critical interpretations.

## Open Science Commitment

All trained model weights, synthetic training corpora, and preprocessing scripts will be deposited in our Zenodo community repository under CC-BY-4.0 licensing within three months of each milestone. Interactive Jupyter notebooks enabling reproduction of every published figure will accompany peer-reviewed manuscripts. We additionally commit to streaming seminar discussions on PlanetaryAI—our nascent federated collaboration platform connecting 47 institutions across fourteen nations.

## Anticipated Deliverables

- Validated THERA classifier achieving ≥94% receiver-operating-characteristic area under curve on held-out synthetic benchmarks
- Retrospective application to Thermal Emission Spectrometer archival observations of Nili Fossae and Jezero palaeolacustrine deposits
- Predictive target-prioritization catalogue for Europa Clipper's E-THEMIS instrument planning cycles
- Two first-author publications in *Astrobiology* and *Planetary Science Journal*

## Why Deep Science Ventures?

DSV's mandate to catalyze transformative, commercially agnostic inquiry aligns perfectly with THERA's positioning at the nexus of artificial intelligence and fundamental planetary exploration. Traditional funding mechanisms inadequately support high-risk methodological innovation lacking immediate terrestrial application. Your portfolio's demonstrated enthusiasm for interdisciplinary convergence makes this partnership intellectually and strategically compelling.

---

**Budget Synopsis:** Personnel (£168,000), GPU cluster allocation (£52,000), laboratory calorimetry consumables (£34,000), open-access publication fees and conferencing (£31,000).

# Grant Application: Astrochemical Cartography via Generative Topological Mapping

**Principal Investigator:** Dr. Yuki Tavares-Okonkwo
**Affiliation:** Meridian Institute for Exoplanetary Geochemistry
**Requested Funding:** £187,000 over 18 months

---

## Executive Summary

Detecting biosignatures within planetary atmospheres demands spectral interpretation frameworks far surpassing conventional retrieval pipelines. We propose **LITHOGEN** (Latent Inference of Thermochemical Habitats via Optimised Generative Ensembles Network), a variational architecture that constructs probabilistic atlases of atmospheric disequilibrium across rocky exoplanets. Rather than classifying individual spectra as biotic or abiotic, LITHOGEN produces continuous manifold representations—astrochemical cartographies—revealing gradients between prebiotic chemistry, geological outgassing, and metabolic signatures.

## Scientific Rationale

Current retrieval codes (e.g., NEMESIS, petitRADTRANS) excel at constraining mixing ratios but struggle with degeneracy when multiple volatile species overlap spectrally. The forthcoming deluge from JWST Mid-Infrared Instrument observations and Ariel's survey catalogue will exacerbate this bottleneck. Meanwhile, agnostic biosignature detection—championed by scholars advocating life-as-we-don't-know-it paradigms—requires moving beyond targeted molecular inventories toward holistic thermodynamic assessments.

Our approach leverages **conditional variational autoencoders** coupled with graph neural networks encoding stoichiometric reaction pathways. Training proceeds on synthetic spectra generated through photochemical kinetics simulations spanning oxic, anoxic, sulfur-rich, and hydrocarbon-dominated regimes. The latent topology self-organizes into chemically meaningful neighbourhoods, enabling anomaly quantification without presupposing terrestrial biochemistry.

## Methodology

**Phase I (Months 1–7):** Construct a heterogeneous training corpus exceeding 2.4 million simulated transmission spectra using VULCAN and KINETICS7 photochemical solvers. Parameterise stellar irradiation (M-dwarf through K-type), surface pressures (0.01–12 bar), and bulk compositions spanning carbon-oxygen ratios from 0.2 to 1.8.

**Phase II (Months 5–13):** Architect and benchmark LITHOGEN against established Bayesian retrieval suites. Validate disequilibrium quantification using Gibbs free energy departure metrics on Venus, Titan, and Archean Earth analogues.

**Phase III (Months 12–18):** Deploy open-access inference endpoints via EOSC-hosted containers. Release curated spectral atlases under CC-BY-4.0 licensing. Publish companion pedagogical notebooks facilitating community adoption.

## Open Science Commitment

Every synthetic dataset, trained checkpoint, and evaluation harness will inhabit a version-controlled Zenodo repository. LITHOGEN's modular codebase adopts Apache 2.0 permissive licensing, encouraging downstream forking by observatory pipelines and citizen-science collectives alike. We will host quarterly virtual hackathons inviting early-career researchers from underrepresented institutions to co-develop auxiliary modules.

## Alignment with DSV Mission

Deep Science Ventures prioritises ventures dissolving entrenched disciplinary barriers. LITHOGEN explicitly merges computational astrochemistry with frontier generative modelling, producing infrastructure transferable to geological exploration, industrial emission fingerprinting, and pharmaceutical spectroscopy. Our deliverables constitute shared epistemic resources rather than proprietary algorithms—catalysing downstream ventures without gatekeeping.

## Budget Highlights

| Category | Allocation |
|---|---|
| GPU cluster compute (A100 nodes) | £62,000 |
| Postdoctoral researcher (astro-informatics) | £78,500 |
| Open-infrastructure hosting & CI/CD | £14,000 |
| Workshop organisation & travel | £18,500 |
| Contingency & dissemination | £14,000 |

## Expected Outcomes

- Peer-reviewed publications in *Nature Astronomy* and *AJ*
- Operational manifold explorer dashboard for community interrogation
- Quantitative taxonomic schema classifying atmospheric archet

# Grant Application: Lithoautotrophic Biosignature Detection via Adversarial Spectral Decomposition

**Principal Investigator:** Dr. Yael Kostenko, Planetary Geobiochemistry Lab, Utrecht Institute for Earth Sciences

**Project Title:** MINERVA — Mining Infrared and Near-UV Emissions for Recognition of Viable Astrobiology

---

## 1. Executive Summary

Subsurface chemoautotrophic metabolisms remain the most thermodynamically plausible candidates for extant biology on Mars, Enceladus, and Europa. Yet current spectroscopic pipelines systematically conflate abiogenic mineral assemblages with genuine metabolic residues, producing unacceptable false-positive rates. MINERVA proposes a generative adversarial framework trained on synthetic reflectance libraries to disambiguate serpentinization-derived organics from authentic lithoautotrophic fingerprints across mid-infrared and vacuum-ultraviolet wavelengths.

## 2. Scientific Rationale

Phyllosilicate-rich terrains on Noachian-era Martian paleolakes exhibit spectral degeneracies between Fe/Mg-smectite overtone absorptions and carboxylate functional groups characteristic of acetogenic metabolism. Conventional principal component analyses collapse these overlapping features into indistinguishable eigenspaces. Meanwhile, Cassini INMS detections of molecular hydrogen in Enceladean plume material hint at active hydrothermal disequilibria capable of sustaining methanogenic archaea analogues — but confirmatory spectral discrimination tools remain embryonic.

Our preliminary work catalogued 14,200 reflectance spectra from analogue sites: Ligurian ophiolites, Samail peridotite aquifers, and Río Tinto acidic ferruginous sediments. Crucially, we identified a previously unreported absorption doublet near 6.85 μm exclusive to biologically precipitated siderite, absent in purely abiotic counterparts. This narrow diagnostic window motivates dedicated algorithmic exploitation.

## 3. Technical Approach

MINERVA integrates three synergistic modules:

**Module A — Spectral Forge:** A conditional variational autoencoder generates physically consistent synthetic spectra parameterized by mineralogy, atmospheric column opacity, temperature gradients, and hypothetical metabolic endmember concentrations. Latent-space interpolation enables continuous exploration of compositional phase boundaries inaccessible through laboratory measurement alone.

**Module B — Adversarial Discriminator:** A Wasserstein GAN architecture, regularized via gradient penalty, distinguishes biogenic from geochemical spectral mimics. The discriminator ingests both fabricated and empirical datacubes, iteratively sharpening its capacity to recognize subtle asymmetries in absorption envelopes that human analysts routinely overlook.

**Module C — Explainability Engine:** SHAP-value attribution maps and attention-weighted saliency overlays ensure every classification decision traces to interpretable physicochemical mechanisms, satisfying reproducibility standards essential for extraordinary claims regarding extraterrestrial biology.

## 4. Open Science Commitment

All training corpora, architectural hyperparameters, and pretrained weights will be deposited in a Zenodo repository under CC-BY-4.0 licensing. We will publish exclusively through diamond open-access journals and maintain a living preprint throughout the project lifecycle. Community annotation sprints, hosted quarterly, will invite planetary scientists globally to validate edge-case classifications.

## 5. Budget Overview (24 months)

| Category | Allocation (GBP) |
|---|---|
| GPU cluster rental (HPC Wales) | £38,000 |
| Postdoctoral researcher (spectroscopy/ML) | £72,000 |
| Fieldwork — analogue specimen acquisition | £15,500 |
| Laboratory FTIR/Raman characterization | £19,000 |
| Conference dissemination & workshops | £5,500 |
| **Total** | **£150,000** |

## 6. Anticipated Impact

MINERVA directly augments interpretation pipelines for ESA's Rosalind Franklin drill-acquired spectra, JWST transiting exoplanet atmospheric retrievals, and forthcoming Europa Clipper MISE observations. Beyond instrumentation support, our adversarial methodology establishes a transferable paradigm: wherever diagnostic amb

Farther afield, Kuiper Belt objects like Arrokoth reveal pristine accretionary architecture unchanged since nebular condensation epochs. Their bilobed contact-binary morphology suggests extraordinarily gentle coalescence velocities, challenging earlier collisional aggregation models that predicted violent fragmentation during planetesimal assembly.

Exoplanetary discoveries continually reshape our taxonomic frameworks. Hot Neptunes occupying the Savanna desert, sub-Saturnian worlds exhibiting radius valley characteristics, and tidally locked lava planets with magmatic hemispheric oceans—none were anticipated before radial velocity surveys commenced decades ago.

Isotopic fractionation patterns measured within chondritic meteorites meanwhile constrain protoplanetary disk thermochemistry, connecting laboratory geochemistry with astronomical observations spanning parsecs. Refractory calcium-aluminum inclusions represent arguably the oldest datable solids, crystallized approximately 4.567 billion years prior.

Planetary science thus weaves mineralogy, fluid mechanics, orbital dynamics, and prebiotic chemistry into one magnificent interdisciplinary tapestry—perpetually expanding as robotic emissaries venture deeper into cosmic wilderness.

text:
# Grant Application: Green Horizons Climate Fund
**Project Title:** Subsurface Thermal Drift Mapping in Post-Industrial Riparian Corridors
**Applicant:** Dr. Mara Voss, Civic Environmental Instrumentation Lab

---

I'll be honest — I'm not the flashiest researcher you'll encounter. But I've spent eleven years crawling through drainage easements with datalogging equipment, and I've noticed something genuinely alarming that nobody's adequately documented.

Urban riverbeds are warming from below.

Specifically, I'm talking about subsurface thermal anomalies in post-industrial riparian zones — places where old manufacturing infrastructure left buried heat sinks that nobody's systematically measured. Our preliminary work along the Kettrick Canal corridor (three sites, eighteen months, embarrassingly underfunded) revealed temperature differentials of 4-7°C between surface water and hyporheic sediment layers. That's striking. Existing municipal water-quality frameworks completely miss this.

We're proposing deployment of 240 distributed piezoelectric thermal probes across fourteen riparian segments within Millhaven's urban watershed. Each probe transmits at six-hour intervals to a mesh-relay network we've already prototyped — admittedly using recycled hardware from a decommissioned air-quality study, which is exactly as unglamorous as it sounds. The resulting dataset would constitute the first longitudinal subsurface thermal record for an industrial-legacy urban watershed in the northeastern region.

Why does this matter for climate adaptation?

Subsurface heat accumulation fundamentally alters macroinvertebrate habitat viability, which cascades into dissolved-oxygen depletion, which destabilizes the entire aquatic food architecture. Cities keep planting bioswales and celebrating. Meanwhile, the riverbed is quietly cooking. Our sensor array would quantify this degradation pathway with enough granularity to actually inform municipal infrastructure remediation decisions — something vague satellite-derived proxies simply cannot accomplish.

The data architecture deserves explanation. We've partnered with Valcrest Urban Analytics (a small consultancy — not a corporate behemoth, I should clarify) to build a publicly accessible geospatial dashboard. Every temperature reading, every anomaly flag, every calibration log will be openly downloadable. No paywalls, no embargoed datasets. I find proprietary environmental data philosophically offensive, honestly.

Budget-wise, we're requesting $187,400 over 26 months. The largest allocation ($94,200) covers probe fabrication and hydrological installation labor — riverbed instrumentation is physically demanding work that requires specialized wetland contractors. Approximately $41,000 funds data infrastructure and dashboard development. The remainder supports two graduate researchers and travel to three regional municipal planning workshops where we'll present actionable remediation guidance. I'm deliberately keeping my own compensation modest because frankly the project itself is compensation enough.

Our measurable deliverables are specific: 240 deployed sensors with 85% uptime reliability, a continuous 24-month thermal dataset, three peer-reviewed articles submitted to hydrological journals, and direct consultation with Millhaven's Department of Watershed Infrastructure on two planned remediation projects already scheduled for 2026.

Urban sustainability conversations keep gravitating toward atmospheric metrics and rooftop photovoltaics. Underground hydrological systems remain chronically under-examined — a gap that's less photogenic but arguably more consequential for long-term ecological resilience.

I genuinely believe this project addresses something real and urgent. I also recognize the competition for your funding is fierce, and I'm aware our lab lacks the institutional prestige of university-affiliated applicants. What we do have: eighteen months of preliminary data, working hardware, established municipal relationships, and a team that thinks subsurface thermal dynamics are genuinely fascinating.

That last part might be our most distinguishing qualification.

**Total Requested:** $187,400
**Project Duration:** 26 months
corpus_texts:
# The Dirt Beneath Our Feet Knows Everything

I've been obsessed lately with soil. Not in a weird way — well, maybe a little weird. A single handful of healthy topsoil contains more microorganisms than there are humans who've ever walked this planet, and that fact genuinely keeps me waking at 3am with my brain buzzing.

We're losing it faster than we can comprehend. Erosion, industrial agriculture, and compaction strip roughly 24 billion tonnes of fertile soil annually from Earth's surface, which means we're essentially gambling our entire food system on the assumption that synthetic fertilizers can compensate indefinitely. They can't. Nitrogen runoff poisons aquatic ecosystems, creates hypoxic dead zones in coastal waters, and contributes to nitrous oxide emissions — a greenhouse gas roughly 300 times more potent than carbon dioxide over a century-long timescale.

Here's what frustrates me most. Regenerative practices aren't novel or experimental; they're ancient. Cover cropping, rotational grazing, and biochar application have centuries of documented success across Indigenous agricultural traditions worldwide, yet mainstream agribusiness treats them like fringe ideology. I've talked with farmers who've transitioned to no-till methods and watched their land's water retention improve dramatically within three seasons. Three seasons. That's nothing in geological terms.

The mycorrhizal networks underneath grasslands and forests genuinely fascinate me because they're essentially the internet before we invented the internet — fungal threads transmitting nutrients and chemical distress signals between trees across vast underground distances. We've been paving over and poisoning this infrastructure for decades without fully understanding what we're dismantling.

Carbon sequestration conversations always pivot toward atmospheric solutions. Direct air capture, solar geoengineering, reforestation initiatives. Important, sure. But soil holds approximately three times more carbon than the atmosphere currently does, making it arguably the most underutilized carbon sink available to us right now, sitting directly beneath our boots.

I don't think hopelessness serves anyone here. Restoration ecology demonstrates repeatedly that degraded landscapes can recover with astonishing resilience when given half a chance. The science is clear, the pathways exist, and the urgency is undeniable. We just need to start treating dirt like it matters — because it unequivocally does.

# The Soil Beneath Our Feet Is Screaming

I've been obsessing over dirt lately. Not metaphorically — actual, crumbling, microbe-packed soil that most people walk across without a second thought, utterly indifferent to the catastrophe unfolding centimeters below their sneakers.

Here's what terrifies me: we're losing topsoil at roughly 24 billion tonnes annually. That number sits in my chest like a stone. Industrial agriculture strips the land of its structural integrity, its fungal networks, its capacity to sequester carbon, and we've essentially normalized this pillaging because crop yields still look acceptable on quarterly reports. We've confused productivity with health, and that distinction is costing us everything.

I've spent hours reading about mycorrhizal fungi — those extraordinary subterranean architects that connect root systems across entire forests, shuttling phosphorus and nitrogen like microscopic postal workers. They're magnificent. Synthetic fertilizers essentially make these networks redundant, so the fungi retreat, the biological complexity collapses, and we're left with chemically dependent monocultures that can't sustain themselves without continuous intervention. It's a dependency spiral with continental consequences.

Regenerative practices exist. Cover cropping, no-till farming, strategic composting, and managed rotational grazing can rebuild degraded soils within decades rather than the millennia natural processes typically require. I'm not romanticizing preindustrial agriculture here — I'm talking about science-backed methodologies that simultaneously restore ecosystem function and maintain viable yields. The evidence is genuinely compelling.

But I keep returning to something uncomfortable. Policy moves glacially while erosion doesn't. Subsidies still overwhelmingly reward conventional tillage operations that pulverize soil structure each planting season. I've read the parliamentary reports, the IPCC chapters, the peer-reviewed journals, and the disconnect between what researchers know and what legislators prioritize remains staggering.

Soil contains more biodiversity than any ecosystem visible above ground. Every tablespoon harbors billions of bacteria, thousands of fungal species, nematodes, protozoa — an entire civilization conducting chemistry that keeps atmospheric carbon from accelerating our warming crisis. We're dismantling this civilization without ceremony, without acknowledgment.

I think that's what unsettles me most. The violence is invisible, so it registers as acceptable. It isn't.

text:
# Grant Application: Urban Thermal Drift Detection Network
**Applicant:** Meridian Watershed Collective | **Requested:** $84,700

---

**Project Summary**

We're chasing something weird. Specifically, I'm talking about the inexplicable thermal discrepancies appearing between Portland's industrial waterfront corridors and its residential canopy zones — discrepancies that nobody's bothered mapping with any real precision, which honestly surprises me given how obvious the problem is.

Our existing instruments are embarrassingly outdated. We've been running three Vaisala HMP110 humidity-temperature probes since 2019, and they're held together partly by zip ties and stubbornness — not exactly inspiring confidence in our dataset integrity.

**The Core Problem We're Addressing**

Portland's urban heat distribution remains poorly characterized. Fourteen distinct microclimatic pockets exist within the city's northeastern quadrant, each behaving idiosyncratically depending on pavement albedo, tree density, and proximity to industrial heat emitters.

Current monitoring captures maybe 40% of meaningful variance. That's a substantial gap, and I won't pretend otherwise.

**What We Actually Plan To Do**

We're deploying 23 LoRaWAN-connected atmospheric sensor clusters. Each cluster integrates particulate measurement, surface radiance detection, and barometric logging simultaneously, transmitting readings every six minutes to our central aggregation hub.

Installation sites aren't random. We selected locations using a stratified sampling methodology developed collaboratively with Dr. Anselm Vogel's geospatial lab at Reed College, prioritizing zones where socioeconomic vulnerability intersects with predicted thermal amplification — because heat kills people, and those people are disproportionately low-income.

**Technical Specifications (Abbreviated)**

The sensor hardware combines Sensirion STS40 precision thermometers with Bosch BME688 environmental units, which measure volatile organic compounds alongside the standard temperature-humidity-pressure triad. Calibration drift gets corrected quarterly through a cross-referencing protocol we've named VERITAS (Variance-Eliminated Recalibration Integration for Thermal Atmospheric Stability) — admittedly a tortured acronym, but the underlying mathematics are solid.

Data pipelines feed directly into our open-access repository. Anyone can pull hourly aggregates without bureaucratic friction.

**Community Integration**

This isn't a study that disappears into academic archives. Neighborhood associations in Cully, Woodlawn, and Overlook have already committed to hosting equipment. Local volunteers — trained through eight-hour certification workshops — will handle basic maintenance and flag anomalous readings.

We're building interpretive dashboards specifically designed for non-specialist audiences. Jargon-free summaries, translated into Spanish and Vietnamese, will circulate monthly through existing community newsletters.

**Budget Transparency**

Hardware procurement runs $51,200. Personnel (myself as project coordinator, one part-time data analyst, community liaison stipends) accounts for $24,500. Remaining funds cover calibration equipment, translation services, and a contingency reserve I'm genuinely hoping we don't need.

**Why This Matters Right Now**

Portland experienced three unprecedented heat events between 2021 and 2024. Existing municipal response relied on temperature data from just seven official stations. That spatial sparsity is dangerous — it masks localized extremes where vulnerable residents face the greatest physiological risk.

Our network would expand monitoring coverage by roughly 340%. The resulting dataset will directly inform city cooling center placement decisions for summer 2026.

**Closing Thought**

I recognize this application competes against projects with shinier branding and more prestigious institutional affiliation. We're a scrappy collective with demonstrable community trust and a genuine problem worth solving. That's the honest pitch — take it or leave it.

text:
# Grant Application: Green Horizons Climate Fund
**Project Title:** Subsurface Hydrological Drift Mapping in Post-Industrial Urban Corridors
**Applicant:** Marcus Delray, Independent Researcher, Portland OR

---

**The Problem I'm (Awkwardly) Trying to Solve**

Nobody watches the groundwater. Seriously, I've checked. Portland's Albina District sits atop fractured basalt aquifers that nobody's instrumented since 1987, and that's genuinely embarrassing for a city this obsessed with sustainability branding. We're flying blind through a climate transition.

Subsurface moisture displacement beneath urban impervious surfaces creates cascading thermal anomalies that distort street-level temperature readings by 2–4°C, depending on precipitation seasonality. That's not a trivial error margin. It corrupts every microclimate model downstream.

**What I'm Actually Proposing**

Fourteen piezometric microsensors. They'd be embedded at staggered depths — 0.8m, 2.1m, and 4.3m — across seven strategically selected parcels throughout the Albina and Eliot neighborhoods. Each unit pairs a Honeywell MPR-series pressure transducer with a HOBO MX2301 temperature/humidity logger transmitting via LoRaWAN to a centralized Raspberry Pi mesh relay I've already partially prototyped (badly, honestly, but it works).

Real-time aquifer pressure differentials would feed a publicly accessible dashboard. Citizens deserve that transparency, and city planners desperately need granular subsurface data when designing permeable pavement retrofits and green stormwater infrastructure.

**Why This Angle Hasn't Been Tried**

Most urban environmental monitoring targets atmospheric conditions. That's intuitive but incomplete. Ignoring subsurface hydrology when modeling urban heat persistence is like diagnosing a fever while ignoring kidney function — you're missing a critical regulatory mechanism.

The Albina corridor also carries significant legacy contamination plumes from defunct dry-cleaning operations and a decommissioned electroplating facility on Killingsworth Street. Those dissolved solvent concentrations migrate unpredictably with climate-driven precipitation intensification. My sensor array would track that migration quarterly, creating the first longitudinal contamination drift dataset for this specific geography.

**My Credentials (Such As They Are)**

I spent six years as a hydrological technician with Cascadia Environmental Consulting before pivoting to independent research, which sounded more romantic than it has proved financially. I've published two technical reports through Oregon DEQ on riparian buffer degradation metrics, and I co-authored a conference paper on soil capillarity modeling that twelve people read (my estimate, possibly generous).

**The Ask**

Requesting $47,800 total. Sensor hardware and installation: $19,200. LoRaWAN gateway infrastructure and licensing: $8,400. Data platform development and public dashboard hosting: $11,600. My own modest stipend for eighteen months of fieldwork and analysis: $8,600 (yes, that's embarrassingly low, but I've budgeted honestly rather than optimistically).

**What Success Looks Like**

By month fourteen, we'd possess a continuous eighteen-month hydrological time series — precipitation events correlated with aquifer pressure responses, thermal anomaly fluctuations mapped spatially across the district, and contaminant plume displacement vectors calculated seasonally. That dataset gets deposited permanently with Portland State University's Urban Studies archive.

**Why This Matters Beyond Portland**

Post-industrial urban districts everywhere share this instrumentation gap. A replicable, affordable sensor methodology developed here gets documented openly for adoption elsewhere. I'm not precious about credit — the data matters more than my byline.

Subsurface monitoring is unglamorous, underfunded, and genuinely necessary. I'd be grateful for the opportunity to prove that.

text:
# Grant Application: Green Horizons Climate Fund
**Project: Distributed Particulate Surveillance in Riparian Corridors**
*Applicant: Mesa Threshold Environmental Collective*

---

**Project Overview**

I'll be upfront — I'm not a polished grant writer. But I've spent three years wading through floodplain sediment with jury-rigged sensors, and that's gotta count for something.

Our proposal centers on deploying a mesh network of low-cost particulate and volatile-organic-compound detectors along the Creston River Basin's twelve tributary corridors, spanning roughly 340 urban kilometers. We're calling it the Riparian Atmospheric Lattice, or RAL — which admittedly sounds like a dental procedure. The instrumentation involves modified Plantower PMS7003 modules paired with proprietary humidity-compensation firmware we've developed (badly, at first, then less badly) over eighteen months of iterative field calibration.

Data flows matter enormously here. Each node transmits readings every four minutes. The aggregated telemetry feeds a PostgreSQL time-series database, triggering anomaly flags when particulate concentrations deviate beyond two standard deviations from localized seasonal baselines. Boring infrastructure? Absolutely. Necessary? Without question.

Urban sustainability demands granular atmospheric portraits, not broad regional averages. Cities breathe unevenly. Emissions cluster near arterial corridors, industrial buffer zones, and — counterintuitively — certain residential composting aggregation sites our preliminary surveys identified last autumn. You can't fix what you can't locate with precision.

The RAL network addresses three intertwined deficiencies in existing monitoring frameworks. First, municipal air-quality stations typically sit kilometers apart, creating observational deserts between measurement points. Second, riparian zones specifically accumulate chemical signatures from both terrestrial runoff and atmospheric deposition simultaneously, making them uniquely informative — yet chronically understudied. Third, real-time public-facing dashboards remain scarce in mid-sized municipalities where advocacy groups most desperately need accessible evidence for zoning negotiations.

I'll admit the dashboard component intimidates me slightly. We've contracted Delphine Vargas, a data-visualization specialist whose previous work mapped stormwater contamination across four Midwestern counties with admirable clarity. She'll make our output legible to city councillors who honestly couldn't distinguish a particulate matter reading from a grocery receipt.

**Budget Justification**

Requested funding: $87,400 over twenty-two months. The largest expenditure covers forty-eight sensor nodes at approximately $340 each, including weatherproofed enclosures rated for sustained precipitation exposure. Personnel constitutes roughly sixty percent of total costs — two part-time field technicians plus Vargas's contracted visualization hours. Equipment doesn't install itself, however much I've fantasized otherwise.

**Expected Deliverables**

We anticipate continuous atmospheric monitoring across all twelve corridors by month nine. Publicly accessible real-time dashboards launch month fourteen. A peer-reviewed methodology paper targeting the *Journal of Urban Atmospheric Research* arrives month twenty-one, assuming reviewers prove merciful.

**Why This Matters**

Climate adaptation without hyperlocal data is essentially guesswork wearing a respectable blazer. Municipalities making infrastructure investments — green corridors, permeable paving, urban canopy expansion — require empirical atmospheric feedback confirming whether interventions actually alter chemical concentrations over time.

The Creston Basin houses approximately 180,000 residents. Many lack political leverage proportional to their exposure to degraded air quality along industrial tributary segments. Our network won't solve those inequities alone — I'm not naive enough to claim otherwise. It will, however, furnish undeniable, continuous, granular evidence that communities and their advocates can wield with confidence.

That strikes me as worthwhile, even if my grant-writing prose remains somewhat embarrassing.

---

*Contact: director@mesathreshold.org | Submitted February 2025*

The science is clear, the pathways exist, and the urgency is undeniable. We just need to start treating dirt like it matters — because it unequivocally does.

# The Soil Beneath Our Feet Is Screaming

I've been obsessing over dirt lately. Not metaphorically — actual, crumbling, microbe-packed soil that most people walk across without a second thought, utterly indifferent to the catastrophe unfolding centimeters below their sneakers.

Here's what terrifies me: we're losing topsoil at roughly 24 billion tonnes annually. That number sits in my chest like a stone. Industrial agriculture strips the land of its structural integrity, its fungal networks, its capacity to sequester carbon, and we've essentially normalized this pillaging because crop yields still look acceptable on quarterly reports. We've confused productivity with health, and that distinction is costing us everything.

I've spent hours reading about mycorrhizal fungi — those extraordinary subterranean architects that connect root systems across entire forests, shuttling phosphorus and nitrogen like microscopic postal workers. They're magnificent. Synthetic fertilizers essentially make these networks redundant, so the fungi retreat, the biological complexity collapses, and we're left with chemically dependent monocultures that can't sustain themselves without continuous intervention. It's a dependency spiral with continental consequences.

Regenerative practices exist. Cover cropping, no-till farming, strategic composting, and managed rotational grazing can rebuild degraded soils within decades rather than the millennia natural processes typically require. I'm not romanticizing preindustrial agriculture here — I'm talking about science-backed methodologies that simultaneously restore ecosystem function and maintain viable yields. The evidence is genuinely compelling.

But I keep returning to something uncomfortable. Policy moves glacially while erosion doesn't. Subsidies still overwhelmingly reward conventional tillage operations that pulverize soil structure each planting season. I've read the parliamentary reports, the IPCC chapters, the peer-reviewed journals, and the disconnect between what researchers know and what legislators prioritize remains staggering.

Soil contains more biodiversity than any ecosystem visible above ground. Every tablespoon harbors billions of bacteria, thousands of fungal species, nematodes, protozoa — an entire civilization conducting chemistry that keeps atmospheric carbon from accelerating our warming crisis. We're dismantling this civilization without ceremony, without acknowledgment.

I think that's what unsettles me most. The violence is invisible, so it registers as acceptable. It isn't.

# Grant Application: Responsible AI Foundation
**Project Title:** Activation Steering Doesn't Generalize the Way We Think It Does

---

I've spent the last eight months running activation steering experiments on Mistral-7B and a fine-tuned variant I call Mistral-7B-Civic (trained on 2.3M tokens of municipal policy text), and I keep hitting the same wall: the steered behaviors that look clean in evaluation don't survive distribution shift. Not even modest shift. Move from the CIVICBENCH-2024 test prompts to real city council transcripts, and the suppression effects I was measuring — average 73.4% reduction in sycophantic framing on held-out prompts — drop to 31.1%. That's not a rounding error. That's a different phenomenon.

I presented a preliminary version of this finding at the ALPS Workshop (Alignment Probing and Latent Spaces, November 2024, co-located with NeurIPS) and got two reactions: "interesting, probably dataset contamination" and "we see this too but haven't written it up." Neither of those responses is sufficient for a safety-relevant technique that people are actively deploying.

What I want to do with this grant is answer a specific question: **are activation steering interventions learning to suppress surface features of the target behavior, or are they restructuring the underlying computation?**

My current hypothesis, based on probing work using the RAVEL toolkit (Ravichander et al., adapted for my pipeline as RAVEL-Steer v0.3), is the former. When I look at the residual stream after steering, the mid-layer representations still cluster in ways that predict the original behavior — they just don't route to it in the evaluation context. The steering vector is functioning more like a mask than a correction. This matters enormously for deployment, because masks fail when the input distribution changes and corrections, in principle, shouldn't.

To test this properly I need three things I don't currently have: compute to run the same analysis across at least four model families (I've budgeted for Llama-3-8B, Gemma-2-9B, Qwen-2.5-7B, and one closed model via API), a structured multi-domain evaluation suite that I'm building out from WILDSTEER-Core plus two new domains I'm collecting specifically for this (legal correspondence and emergency services dispatch logs, both sourced through existing IRB-approved agreements), and time — meaning salary support so I can stop consulting two days a week.

The deliverables are concrete. By month six: a replication dataset (STEER-SHIFT v1.0) released publicly with full documentation. By month ten: a causal mediation analysis comparing pre- and post-steering computation paths across all four model families. By month twelve: a revised evaluation protocol I'm calling SteeredBehaviorRobustness (SBR), designed to catch the generalization failures I've been documenting, submitted to ICLR 2026 or equivalent.

I'm not claiming activation steering is broken. I'm claiming we're measuring it wrong and that the measurement gap has safety implications. If the technique is being used to suppress deceptive or harmful outputs in deployed systems, and those suppressions are masks rather than corrections, then the systems are behaving safely on the test distribution and unsafely everywhere else. That's the scenario we should be most afraid of.

**Requested amount:** $87,400 (12 months, 0.6 FTE salary + compute + data collection)

---

*Contact:* [applicant details omitted per blind review guidelines]

# Why My Loss Curves Keep Lying to Me

I've been staring at the same validation loss plateau for three weeks now, and I'm starting to think I've been measuring the wrong thing entirely.

Here's what happened. I was running RLHF experiments using our internal toolkit — we call it **Shepherd v2.3** — trying to get a language model to refuse requests that combine benign-looking surface features with genuinely harmful intent. Standard stuff, or so I thought. My reward model was hitting 94.7% accuracy on the held-out evaluation set. The training curves looked gorgeous. I was ready to write it up.

Then a colleague, Priya, asked me to run the model against **AdversarialBench-7**, a stress-testing suite we borrowed from Redwood's methodology and modified pretty heavily. The refusal rate on that suite dropped to 61.3%. Not catastrophic, but deeply embarrassing given how confident I'd been.

The core problem, as best I can diagnose it, is that I'd essentially trained a very sophisticated pattern-matcher. The model learned to key on surface-level harm indicators — certain vocabulary clusters, explicit request structures — rather than developing anything resembling robust intent-modeling. When AdversarialBench-7 wrapped harmful requests in unfamiliar linguistic packaging, the refusal behavior collapsed.

What frustrates me is that this isn't a new failure mode. The literature documents it constantly. I knew about it. I still fell into it, because my evaluation suite wasn't adversarial enough to expose the brittleness until Priya pushed me.

I've been thinking about this in terms of what I'm calling the **evaluation-deployment gap**: the distributional distance between whatever you test against and whatever the model eventually encounters. Every safety metric I report lives inside that gap. The real risk isn't the number I measured — it's everything that number doesn't see.

I'm rebuilding the evaluation pipeline now, pulling in more diverse adversarial templates and running systematic perturbation tests using **LinguisticShift 1.1**, a tool we've been developing to generate semantically-equivalent paraphrases with controlled surface variation. Early results suggest my 94.7% was really more like 78.2% under realistic distributional shift.

Which is humbling. But I'd rather know.

# Why My Calibration Scores Keep Humbling Me

I've been running the same elicitation battery for three weeks now, and I still can't get PRISM-7's uncertainty estimates to behave. The tool flags overconfidence at a threshold of 0.73 on the Brier decomposition, and my current model family is sitting at 0.81. That gap is embarrassing, honestly.

The thing that's bothering me isn't the number itself — it's what's underneath it. I've been chasing a specific failure mode where the system expresses high confidence on counterfactual queries involving norm violations. Feed it a scenario where deception is instrumentally useful, and the confidence scores barely budge. They should budge. That's the whole point.

My colleague Priya ran a parallel experiment using SpectraEval's adversarial perturbation suite last month and found something adjacent: her models showed 23.4% higher resistance to confidence degradation on exactly the query types I care about. Twenty-three percent. We had a long argument over coffee about whether that's a calibration artifact or something more structural. I'm still not sure she's wrong.

What I keep returning to is the baseline problem. I'm using Hendricks & Mao's 2022 threat taxonomy as my reference frame, and it's starting to feel like a worn map — the territory has shifted but I haven't updated the cartography. Specifically, the taxonomy doesn't have clean categories for what I'd call "soft norm erosion," where the model doesn't do anything dramatically unsafe but incrementally drifts toward treating constraints as negotiable. My PRISM-7 runs are surfacing this constantly at the 0.4-second response latency window and I don't have good language for it yet.

I'm planning to rebuild the evaluation pipeline around a tighter coupling between the uncertainty module and the behavioral trace logger. The current 847ms lag between those two systems is almost certainly introducing noise I'm attributing to model behavior. That's probably accounting for at least some of the 0.81 score.

Maybe. Or maybe the models are actually just poorly calibrated on the queries that matter most, and I've been too eager to blame my tools. Both things can be true simultaneously, which is the most frustrating feature of this kind of work.

text:
# Grant Application — Responsible AI Foundation
**Applicant:** Dr. Miriam Osei-Bonsu, Computational Cognition Lab, University of Utrecht
**Requested Amount:** $187,400 (18 months)

---

## When Models Lie About What They Don't Know: Closing the Confidence-Calibration Gap in Multi-Step Reasoning

---

I've spent the last two years watching language models confidently walk off cliffs. Not metaphorically — I mean watching them chain together four or five reasoning steps, get step three subtly wrong, and then present the final answer with *higher* expressed confidence than they'd have shown for a one-step version of the same question. That pattern showed up consistently in my preliminary work with the **CASPAR-7 benchmark** (a multi-hop arithmetic-and-causal reasoning suite I assembled from Dutch secondary education records and a subset of the ReasonBank corpus), and it's what's driving this proposal.

The core finding, which I presented at EWAF '23 in Winterthur, is this: across eleven open-weight models ranging from 3B to 70B parameters, expressed confidence (measured via verbalized probability elicitation using the **ConfElicit v2 protocol**) *increases* by an average of 14.3 percentage points when reasoning chains exceed four steps, regardless of whether accuracy holds up. Accuracy, meanwhile, drops by 22.7 points over that same threshold. I'm calling this the confidence-accuracy inversion zone, and it scares me more than raw error rates do, because it's precisely where human oversight is most likely to fail.

What I don't yet have is a mechanistic account of *why* this happens. That's what this grant funds.

The plan has three phases. First, I'll extend CASPAR-7 with adversarially constructed chains where I know exactly which step introduces the error — something I can control because I'm generating the problems synthetically with **StepForge**, a constraint-based problem generator I built in 2022. This gives me ground truth about error location, which most benchmarks don't have. Second, I'll run activation patching experiments using **TransparentLens**, a fork of the TransformerLens library I've been maintaining with two grad students, to identify whether the confidence inflation is driven by attention patterns at the final token position or whether it accumulates across the chain. My hypothesis, based on some ugly preliminary logit-lens plots I showed at the Utrecht AI Safety Reading Group last October, is that it's the latter — that the model is essentially compressing its own uncertainty signal as chain length grows, not inflating it at the end.

Third — and this is the part I'm most uncertain about, honestly — I want to test whether simple interventions at inference time can interrupt this pattern. Specifically, I'll evaluate whether inserting structured self-questioning prompts at each reasoning step (adapted from the **PAUSE-and-Check** framework developed by Lindqvist & Ramos, 2022) reduces the inversion effect without wrecking throughput.

The deliverables are concrete: an extended CASPAR-7 release with step-level error annotations, three peer-reviewed papers targeting ICLR and ACL venues, and a practical calibration audit toolkit that practitioners can run against their own fine-tuned models in under an hour.

I'm not promising I'll solve confidence calibration. What I'm promising is that by the end of eighteen months we'll know *where* in a model's forward pass this problem lives, and that's the prerequisite for fixing it.

text:
# Grant Application: Responsible AI Foundation
**Project Title:** When Models Lie Confidently: Mapping the Failure Modes of Calibration in Safety-Critical Contexts

---

I've been staring at the same scatter plot for six months. It shows prediction confidence against actual accuracy for a family of instruction-tuned models across 14 safety-relevant task domains, and the pattern is genuinely unsettling: models become *more* confidently wrong precisely in the domains where being wrong matters most — medical triage classification, legal risk assessment, crisis hotline response routing.

This isn't a new observation in the abstract. But the specificity is new. Using the **SENTINEL-2 behavioral audit suite** (developed collaboratively with colleagues at the Krakow Uncertainty Lab), I've tracked this inverse-calibration phenomenon across 847 distinct failure instances, with a mean overconfidence delta of 23.7 percentage points in high-stakes domains versus 6.2 points in low-stakes control tasks. I presented preliminary findings at the **Empirical Alignment Workshop, Prague 2023**, and the discussion afterward convinced me the problem is both more tractable and more urgent than the literature suggests.

Here's what I think is actually happening. Current calibration benchmarks — ECE scores, reliability diagrams — measure *average* calibration across held-out distributions. But safety-critical deployment isn't an average situation. It's the tail. And in the tail, the training signal that built these models' confidence estimates is thinnest precisely where consequence is heaviest. I've been calling this the **calibration-consequence inversion**, and I want to build a rigorous empirical and theoretical account of it.

The proposed work has three phases over 18 months.

**Phase 1** (months 1–6): I'll extend the SENTINEL-2 suite to generate what I'm calling *consequence-stratified calibration profiles* — essentially, calibration curves conditioned on domain-expert severity ratings rather than on distribution shift magnitude alone. I'll work with the **MedSafe-Annotated** corpus (9,400 annotated clinical scenarios) and a newly assembled legal-risk dataset I've been building with public defenders in Kraków and Warsaw.

**Phase 2** (months 7–12): Mechanism analysis. I want to know *where in the forward pass* miscalibration crystallizes for high-stakes inputs. Using activation patching across the **Mirador-7B** and **Mirador-13B** model family (interpretability-friendly architecture, released under open weights last March), I'll attempt to localize the representational features that correlate with confidence miscalibration in consequence-heavy contexts. This is technically hard and I might not fully crack it, but partial localization would still be publishable and practically useful.

**Phase 3** (months 13–18): Evaluation framework. The deliverable I care most about is a practitioner-usable evaluation protocol — something a hospital system or crisis intervention platform could actually run before deployment. Not another leaderboard. A diagnostic workflow with decision thresholds and interpretable outputs. I'll pilot it with two NGO partners already expressing interest.

**Budget request: $87,400.** This covers a 0.6 FTE research assistant (12 months), annotation costs for the legal-risk dataset (~$14,200 estimated), compute, and travel to share the framework at two venues.

I'm not promising the calibration problem is solved at the end of this. I'm promising we'll understand it better and have something practitioners can use. That feels like the right kind of commitment to make honestly.

---

*Applicant: Dr. Monika Zawadzki, Krakow Uncertainty Lab*
*Contact: m.zawadzki@kul-research.pl*

text:
**Application to the Responsible AI Foundation — Cycle 4**
**Project: Calibration Drift Under Distribution Shift: Building a Continuous Monitoring Framework for Deployed Alignment Interventions**

---

I've spent the last eighteen months watching alignment techniques break in slow motion.

The specific problem I keep running into: RLHF-tuned and DPO-trained models look great on held-out evals at deployment time, then quietly drift in their calibration properties over the following weeks as the input distribution shifts. Nobody catches it until something goes wrong. I first noticed this pattern while auditing logs from a mid-scale production assistant (three million monthly active users, anonymized under NDA) and confirmed it against the HeliosBench-22 dataset I assembled from 140,000 timestamped user sessions across six application domains. The core finding — that alignment-tuned models show measurable confidence miscalibration within 14.3 ± 2.1 days of novel-domain traffic exposure — shaped my talk at the Cascadia AI Safety Symposium in March 2024, and the reaction in that room told me this is a problem the field recognizes but hasn't systematically addressed.

What I'm proposing is a monitoring framework called **DriftWatch** that sits between a deployed model and its traffic pipeline, tracking a set of alignment-specific calibration signals rather than just accuracy or refusal rates. The existing monitoring literature (mostly borrowed from MLOps) watches for covariate shift in input features. That's insufficient here because alignment failures aren't primarily about what the model *sees* — they're about how the model's value-relevant behavior shifts relative to its stated uncertainty. DriftWatch tracks three signal families: (1) expected calibration error on safety-adjacent outputs, decomposed by semantic cluster using the HDBSCAN-based clustering I developed in the Meridian toolkit; (2) divergence between the model's verbalized confidence and its token-probability distribution on contested normative claims; and (3) temporal autocorrelation in refusal-adjacent activations, extracted via a lightweight linear probe trained on the final four residual stream layers.

I've already built a working prototype. On the HeliosBench-22 data, DriftWatch flagged calibration degradation with 0.847 AUROC against hand-labeled "alignment slip" events, compared to 0.612 for standard population stability index monitoring and 0.703 for the baseline ECE-only approach. The false positive rate at a 95% true positive threshold was 11.3%, which is high enough that I know I need to do more work on it — and that's part of what this grant would fund.

The eighteen-month roadmap has three phases. First, I'll extend DriftWatch to cover instruction-following drift separately from value-calibration drift, because I think conflating these is producing noisy signals. Second, I'll run a prospective study with two partner organizations (one fintech application, one legal research tool, both under signed data-sharing agreements) to get ground-truth labeled drift events in real deployment conditions. Third, I'll package the whole system with documentation and a calibration dataset so other researchers can actually use it, because right now the interpretability community builds beautiful tools that require a PhD to run.

The budget request is $94,000 over 18 months: $62,000 salary (myself, 0.7 FTE), $18,000 compute (primarily activation extraction and probe training at scale), $9,000 data agreements and annotation, $5,000 conference dissemination.

This isn't a theoretical project. I have data, a prototype, and two partners waiting. What I don't have is the runway to finish it properly.

text:
# Grant Application — Responsible AI Foundation
**Project: Measuring What Models Won't Say: Refusal Topology as an Alignment Signal**

---

I've spent the last fourteen months cataloging refusals.

Not because I planned to — I was originally building REFMAP, a structured dataset of ~41,000 model refusals across six production-deployed systems, to help a collaborator study jailbreak surface area. But somewhere around month three, staring at a cluster analysis that wouldn't behave, I noticed something that's been eating at me ever since: *refusals have internal geometry*, and that geometry is telling us things we're ignoring.

Here's what I mean. When I ran UMAP projections on refusal embeddings from four RLHF-trained models (using the intermediate-layer activations I extract with RepLens v0.7, my own lightweight toolkit), the refusal space wasn't uniform. It stratified. There were tight, high-confidence clusters — "won't help with weapons synthesis," essentially invariant — and then these sprawling, diffuse regions I started calling *contested zones*, where the model's behavior was inconsistent across semantically near-identical prompts. A 0.91 cosine similarity between two prompts predicting opposite refusal outcomes in 34.7% of cases in the GPT-class models I was testing.

That 34.7% is where I think the real alignment signal lives.

I presented a rough version of this framing at the Bay Area Interpretability Symposium in March (BAIS 2024, session 4B) and the pushback I got was useful: people wanted to know if contested zones were measuring *value uncertainty* in the model or just *training noise*. Honest answer: I don't know yet. That's what this proposal is for.

The project has three phases. First, I want to extend REFMAP to 120,000 examples and add adversarial perturbation traces — controlled edits that cross refusal boundaries — so I can map the topology more precisely than I could with the original dataset's opportunistic sampling. Second, I'll use RepLens to extract the activation trajectories of contested-zone responses and compare them against known circuits associated with uncertainty representation (building on the sparse autoencoder work from the Anthropic interpretability team, which gives me a baseline for what "the model doesn't know what to do" looks like mechanistically). Third — and this is the part I'm most uncertain about but most excited by — I want to test whether contested-zone density at deployment time is a leading indicator of alignment drift. I have a tentative collaboration with two researchers at a mid-size AI lab (details in the supplementary) who can give me access to logged refusal behavior across a six-month model lifecycle. If contested zones expand before human raters notice behavioral degradation, that's a real evaluation signal.

The practical output: a public refusal topology benchmark (RTBench-1) and a documented methodology that evaluation teams can actually run on their own systems without needing my specific toolkit. I've been burned by irreproducible interpretability results before — I'm building this so someone can check my work with a different embedder and tell me I'm wrong.

Budget ask: $94,200 over 18 months. Roughly 60% is my time (I'm currently unaffiliated, which is awkward but also means I can move fast), 25% is compute for the activation extraction work, and 15% covers a part-time research assistant for dataset annotation quality control.

I think refusal behavior is underused as an alignment diagnostic. I'd like the chance to find out if I'm right.
